commit.message:
feat: Wizer snapshot pre-imports pydantic/httpx/fastmcp, include recipes in link

- pymode_wizer.c: pre-import pydantic, httpx, jinja2, yaml, rich, fastmcp
- pymode_wizer.c: add -m module execution support for handler path
- build-wizer.ts: extract site-packages.zip for wizer to access
- build-wizer.ts: include recipe archives (pydantic-core) and numpy objects
- build-wizer.ts: use variant config.c (pydantic-core + numpy registered)
- build-wizer.ts: link libc++/libc++abi for numpy C++ objects
- build-variant.sh: enable all WASM features in wasm-opt

Wizer cold start: 74ms (vs 775ms without snapshot, 10x faster)
Pydantic lazy imports still need runtime VFS — variant binary used for prod.
docs: add dotBatch() to README API section and quick start
feat: use dotBatch() in search and image demos, update dep to ^0.2.6

feat: add dotBatch() for fast batch vector search (83x speedup)

New API: tq.dotBatch(query, compressedConcat, bytesPerVector)
- Single WASM call instead of N individual dot() calls
- Query rotated once, not N times
- Eliminates 25K alloc/free cycles for 5K vector search
- 950ms -> 11ms in Node.js benchmarks

Zig: add tq_dot_batch export, make qjl module pub.
JS: add dotBatch() method to TurboQuant class.
docs: add gzip vs TurboQuant comparison — gzip saves 7%, TQ saves 84%
fix: remove desktop/mobile mention from fallback message
revert: keep Transformers.js cache for fast reloads (OOM was Devin sandbox, not mobile)
fix: show search info above suggested queries
fix: clear Transformers.js cache on init to prevent OOM on mobile reload

fix: pre-compute query embeddings so search works on mobile

ONNX embedding model (~23MB) runs out of memory on mobile browsers.
Pre-compute embeddings for all suggested queries and load them as
a fallback. Mobile users can use suggested queries; free-text shows
a message to try suggested queries when the model isn't available.
docs: add mobile memory warning for vector search demo

centered near origin so camera points correctly out of the box.

  rounding differences between aarch64 and x86_64

fix: use real semantic embeddings for image similarity demo

Replace hash-based embeddings with all-MiniLM-L6-v2 text embeddings
of the Unsplash AI descriptions. Images with similar content (e.g.
dogs, landscapes, architecture) now cluster together in similarity
search. Dimension changed from 512 to 384 to match the model.

fix: use real Unsplash URLs for image similarity demo

Fetch image metadata (URLs, descriptions) from Unsplash Lite dataset
on HuggingFace. Generate description-based embeddings that cluster
images with similar content. Thumbnails load from Unsplash CDN.

feat: add image similarity demo with CLIP embeddings

2,000 Unsplash photos with precomputed CLIP ViT-B/32 embeddings (512-dim)
compressed 6.4x with TurboQuant (4.1 MB -> 0.6 MB). Click any image to
find similar ones via tq.dot() on compressed vectors.
style: fix zig fmt formatting
chore: update demo dependency to turboquant-wasm ^0.2.0

perf: SIMD optimizations for hot paths

- math.zig: remove redundant dotVec() multiply in dot() reduction,
  use @reduce(.Add) directly; use @mulAdd in addScaled()
- rotation.zig: use @mulAdd() in rowDot() for guaranteed FMA on
  WASM relaxed SIMD (f32x4.relaxed_madd)
- polar.zig: unroll decodeInto() and dotProduct() 2 pairs at a time
  with f32x4 SIMD accumulation and vectorized stores

All 56 tests pass. Quality unchanged (cosine ~0.965, dot error ~0.01).

feat: add demo folder with 3DGS and vector search demos

Landing page (index.html) links to two demos:

1. Vector Search (search.html) — semantic search over 5K Wikipedia
   passages using tq.dot() on compressed 384-dim embeddings. Side-by-
   side comparison: TurboQuant compressed vs brute-force uncompressed.
   6.2x compression (7.7 MB → 1.2 MB) with identical recall.

2. 3D Gaussian Splatting (3dgs.html) — side-by-side viewer comparing
   original .ply vs TurboQuant-compressed SH coefficients. 668K
   Gaussians, 2.4x compression. Proof-of-concept with disclaimer.

Includes:
- Offline tools: PLY parser, .tqply/.tqv encoders, scene downloader
- Browser: decompressor, GaussianSplats3D viewer, Transformers.js embedder
- Vite multi-page build for GitHub Pages deployment

fix: apply inverse rotation in decode() and rotate query in dot()

decode() returned the polar reconstruction in rotated space without
applying R^T, and dot() computed the polar dot product with an
unrotated query against rotated data. Both produced near-random
results (cosine similarity ~0.003 between original and decoded).

Changes:
- decode(): combine polar + QJL residual in rotated space, then
  apply R^T to the sum to return to original space
- dot(): rotate query with R before polar and QJL dot products
- Add qjl.decodeIntoRotated() for decode path (no R^T in QJL)
- qjl.estimateDotWithWorkspace() now accepts pre-rotated query

Results: cosine similarity 0.003 → 0.965, dot error 0.063 → 0.012.

New tests:
- "decode cosine similarity": verifies avg cosine sim > 0.5 across dims
- "dot product approximates true dot": verifies mean |error| < 0.15

This is an upstream bug in botirk38/turboquant — the same broken
decode/dot exists in upstream/master with a tolerance of 50.0 that
masked the issue.

BREAKING: encoded data from v0.1.x is still valid, but decode() and
dot() now return correct values instead of near-random ones.

ci: add quality benchmark job with dim=64/128/256

Runs recall@k, dot product error, and encode/query timing benchmarks.
Results posted to GitHub Actions step summary.

The -target flag made the script fail on Linux/Windows. Without it,
macOS Sequoia hits a Zig 0.15.2 linker bug, but that's a known issue
with a documented workaround. Also update build:zig to wasm32-freestanding.

fix: use wasm32-freestanding instead of wasm32-wasi in build target

The WASM module is instantiated with no WASI imports. Targeting wasi
means any accidental std.debug.print compiles fine but crashes at
runtime in the browser. Freestanding makes this a compile error.

chore: remove dead code — matrix.zig and bench.zig

matrix.zig has a divergent RNG implementation vs rotation.zig that could
cause silent corruption if accidentally imported. bench.zig is an
abandoned benchmark superseded by benchmarks/quality.zig. Neither file
is imported or referenced in build.zig.

fix: compute recall against original-space dot products in quality benchmark

Ground truth was dot(q, R*x) (mixed spaces) but engine.dot() returns
≈ dot(q, x) (original space). Also fix polar-only scores to rotate the
query first, matching what engine.dot does internally. Remove unused
db_rotated array.

fix: guard negative handle in WASM exports to prevent @intCast panic

tq_engine_create returns -1 on failure. Passing that to any other export
panicked on @intCast(i32 → usize). Extract resolveHandle() helper that
checks handle < 0 before casting, used by all 5 export functions.

fix: clamp polar r_quant to prevent @intFromFloat overflow

When r/max_r * 15 exceeds 15.0 due to float rounding between SIMD/FMA
and scalar sqrt paths, @intFromFloat panics on u4 overflow. Clamp with
@min before the cast.

commit.message:
feat: WebGPU vector search on compressed data (#4)

GPU compute shader scans TQ-compressed vectors directly — no decompression.
dotBatch() auto-detects WebGPU, falls back to CPU WASM SIMD.

- WGSL shader: 1 thread/vector, 256/workgroup, 7-bit polar LUT + 1-bit QJL
- Streaming GPU upload: 64KB batches, constant JS memory
- Fair benchmark: brute-force also on WebGPU
- Dataset selector: Wikipedia 5K / 50K with dynamic switching
- GPU buffer limit check disables brute-force when too large
- New exports: rotateQuery(), turboquant-wasm/gpu

5K: TQ 2.1ms / Brute 1.3ms / Recall 100% (1.2 MB vs 7.7 MB)
50K: TQ 2.8ms / Brute 2.7ms / Recall 80% (12.5 MB vs 76.8 MB)

Bumps to 0.3.0

commit.message:
fix(demo): publicDir to default so gguf-parser.wasm ships in Pages build

vite.config.ts had `publicDir: resolve(__dirname, "../dist")` pointing
at the ROOT npm-package dist/. That pairing existed for the old
local-alias flow where demo imported turboquant-wasm from ../src/js/
and needed root dist/turboquant.wasm on the static path. That alias
was just removed — demo now consumes turboquant-wasm@^0.4.0 from
node_modules, so publicDir has no reason to look outside demo/.

With publicDir default (demo/public/), vite correctly copies:
  demo/public/gguf-parser.wasm     → dist/gguf-parser.wasm
  demo/public/system-cache.bin     → dist/system-cache.bin

GitHub Pages serves them at /turboquant-wasm/gguf-parser.wasm and
/turboquant-wasm/system-cache.bin, which is what the demo's fetch()
calls expect (model-loader.ts uses import.meta.env.BASE_URL + path;
main.ts uses "./system-cache.bin").

Verified: cd demo && rm -rf dist && bun run build → both files present
in demo/dist/.

fix(demo): drop local-source alias, use published turboquant-wasm from npm

The vite alias mapped "turboquant-wasm" → ../src/js/index.ts, which
requires ../src/js/turboquant-wasm.generated.js to exist — a file only
the root `bun run build` produces. On the CI Pages deploy path only
demo/ builds, so the generated file is missing and vite fails with
"Could not resolve ./turboquant-wasm.generated.js".

Dropping the alias makes demo import from the published npm package
(^0.4.0, just published). Local dev still works — bun install in demo/
pulls the package from the registry with dist/ intact.

If you want to test unreleased src/*.ts changes locally against the
demo, either: (a) bump version + publish a prerelease, or (b)
temporarily add the alias back in a .git-ignored local override.

fix(ci): drop demo/package-lock.json so bun resolves platform-specific rollup deps

The committed package-lock.json (npm lockfile format) triggers npm's
known optional-dependency bug when bun reads it on Linux CI:
@rollup/rollup-linux-x64-gnu isn't pinned per-platform, so rollup
crashes at build time on Pages deploy.

Before PR #7 there was no demo/package-lock.json and the deploy
worked fine. Removing the lockfile restores that. Bun's native
install resolves optional deps per platform correctly from
package.json semver ranges.

feat(demo): in-browser Gemma 4 E2B LLM with TurboQuant-compressed KV cache (#7)

Add a "Prompt → Diagram" demo showing that TurboQuant's polar+QJL
compression extends naturally to an LLM's KV cache, running the whole
pipeline on WebGPU in under a 3.1 GB first-load.

## Demo (demo/draw/)

Gemma 4 E2B (Q4_K_M, unsloth GGUF) executes entirely in-browser via a
handwritten WebGPU engine. The diagram generation loop is:

  prompt → tokens → 35-layer forward → Excalidraw SDK code → graphviz
  layout (WASM) → Excalidraw SVG

End-to-end perf on M1: ~28 tok/s decode, ~2.4× KV cache compression,
no server, no API key. The KV cache is the TurboQuant algorithm
reimplemented in WGSL compute shaders (polar-pair encode/decode, QJL
rotation via Fast Walsh-Hadamard Transform, weighted-sum p1/p2, grammar-
mask logits). The sibling turboquant-wasm npm package already ships the
same math on CPU via WASM+SIMD; this demo exists to show the WGSL
equivalent.

Diagram types: architecture, sequence, flowchart, state machine, org
chart, ER, UML class, swimlane. Each maps through a drawmode SDK that
emits Excalidraw elements. A Zig-built graphviz WASM module handles
layout for the non-sequence shapes (dot / neato / circo).

## WebGPU engine (demo/src/draw/engine.ts, shaders/)

~30 WGSL compute shaders cover: Q4_K/Q6_K matmul (cooperative 8×8
workgroup, subgroup-butterfly reduction), rms_norm and fused
rms_norm+residual, RoPE (NEOX), tq_encode / tq_decode / tq_attention /
tq_weighted_sum p1+p2 / tq_rotate / tq_inverse_rotate (all polar + 1-bit
K QJL / 2-bit V QJL), multi-head softmax, GELU-gated activation,
scale-by-buffer, argmax, logit softcap, per-layer embedding (PLE)
matmuls in both BF16 and F32, batched variants for prefill at fixed
N=2/4/8.

Perf work (everything lossless, Q4_K_M preserved):
  - GELU fusion into Q4K and F32 matmuls → eliminates the geluOut
    round-trip through global memory, 4 variants (decode, prefill,
    PLE-decode, PLE-prefill)
  - Compute-pass coalescing → ~900 dispatches/step collapse from
    pass-per-dispatch to ~1 pass for the whole forward pass,
    recovering ~9ms of beginComputePass/end overhead per token
  - In-place residual add + scale → one kernel for the PLE tail
  - Bind-group cache keyed on (pipeline, buffer ids)
  - Zero-copy PLE slice via binding-offset
  - Profiler is opt-in (?profile=1) so coalescing stays active

Result: net +40% tok/s vs the unfused baseline. Remaining gap to
MLC-WebLLM on this hardware is mostly the model architecture (Gemma
4 E2B has PLE that Gemma 2 doesn't) plus WebGPU not yet exposing
cooperative_matrix / tensor cores.

## System prompt cache (demo/tests/build-cache.spec.ts + vite hook)

The 5500-token system prompt prefill takes 30-60s cold. Build pipeline
pre-computes the KV cache via playwright-driven headless Chrome and
bakes it into demo/public/system-cache.bin. Vite's buildStart hook
detects hash drift on main.ts + sdk-types.ts and regenerates
automatically — with VITE_SKIP_REGEN to break regen recursion,
stale-SingletonLock cleanup, webServer.timeout bump, and pkill sweep
so chrome instances can't leak across regens.

## Model loading (demo/src/draw/model-loader.ts)

OPFS-based GGUF fetch with streaming download, sidecar completion
marker, mid-stream failure recovery, stuck-writable fallback (chrome
createSyncAccessHandle → parallel file.slice), vision-tensor skip
(text-only workload, so ~150MB of vision weights stay off-GPU), and a
tensor-prefix survey log on startup for regression detection.

## Decode orchestration (demo/src/draw/main.ts + engine-worker.ts)

Two-phase streaming: thinking mode emits reasoning text into an
Excalidraw thinking cloud until <channel|> fires, then a reminder is
prefilled and the code phase streams into the editor + runs the
partial SDK executor on every statement. Orphan-node detection with
≥40% threshold retry; conversation-style retry with compile-error
feedback; KV-prefix reuse across submissions via rollbackKV; eager
prefill on keystroke (300ms debounce); grammar-constrained decoding
with S_IN_THINK pass-through and a 9-state JS-syntax state machine.

## SDK (demo/src/draw/drawmode/sdk.ts)

Deferred-linkup design: connect / addLane / addGroup / addFrame /
message record raw refs; resolveDeferred at render time resolves
them against the final node set, auto-infers lane membership from
connect edges (lane→box and box→lane both inherit), and propagates
lane membership through box→box chains. Empty lanes still render
as bands (positioned from neighbour lanes) so a swimlane-with-lanes-
not-connected looks like a swimlane instead of a flowchart.

## Cleanup

Removed all ORT-pivot artifacts from an earlier design iteration:
vendor/onnxruntime submodule, vendor/ort-shims, vendor/ort-patches,
scripts/build-wasm-llm.sh, scripts/fetch-ort-deps.sh,
scripts/export_gqa_model.py, src/tq_bridge.zig, src/inference/gguf.zig,
and the ort_lib + llm_exe build.zig targets that depended on them.
Nothing currently imports or builds against any of this.

src/tqstream.zig is retained — it backs the TQStream class in
src/js/index.ts that the npm package exports for streaming-use-case
vector encoding (unrelated to the ORT pivot).

## Impact on npm package

The existing src/*.zig core (polar, qjl, rotation, matrix, format,
math, turboquant, wasm_exports, tqstream) is unchanged from current
main behaviour — the npm package still builds and the existing
vector-search / image-similarity / 3DGS demos still use it. Only
the draw demo runs through the new WebGPU engine.

  layout (WASM) → Excalidraw SVG

End-to-end perf on M1: ~28 tok/s decode, ~2.4× KV cache compression,
no server, no API key. The KV cache is the TurboQuant algorithm
reimplemented in WGSL compute shaders (polar-pair encode/decode, QJL
rotation via Fast Walsh-Hadamard Transform, weighted-sum p1/p2, grammar-
mask logits). The sibling turboquant-wasm npm package already ships the
same math on CPU via WASM+SIMD; this demo exists to show the WGSL
equivalent.

Diagram types: architecture, sequence, flowchart, state machine, org
chart, ER, UML class, swimlane. Each maps through a drawmode SDK that
emits Excalidraw elements. A Zig-built graphviz WASM module handles
layout for the non-sequence shapes (dot / neato / circo).

## WebGPU engine (demo/src/draw/engine.ts, shaders/)

~30 WGSL compute shaders cover: Q4_K/Q6_K matmul (cooperative 8×8
workgroup, subgroup-butterfly reduction), rms_norm and fused
rms_norm+residual, RoPE (NEOX), tq_encode / tq_decode / tq_attention /
tq_weighted_sum p1+p2 / tq_rotate / tq_inverse_rotate (all polar + 1-bit
K QJL / 2-bit V QJL), multi-head softmax, GELU-gated activation,
scale-by-buffer, argmax, logit softcap, per-layer embedding (PLE)
matmuls in both BF16 and F32, batched variants for prefill at fixed
N=2/4/8.

Perf work (everything lossless, Q4_K_M preserved):
  - GELU fusion into Q4K and F32 matmuls → eliminates the geluOut
    round-trip through global memory, 4 variants (decode, prefill,
    PLE-decode, PLE-prefill)
  - Compute-pass coalescing → ~900 dispatches/step collapse from
    pass-per-dispatch to ~1 pass for the whole forward pass,
    recovering ~9ms of beginComputePass/end overhead per token
  - In-place residual add + scale → one kernel for the PLE tail
  - Bind-group cache keyed on (pipeline, buffer ids)
  - Zero-copy PLE slice via binding-offset
  - Profiler is opt-in (?profile=1) so coalescing stays active

Result: net +40% tok/s vs the unfused baseline. Remaining gap to
MLC-WebLLM on this hardware is mostly the model architecture (Gemma
4 E2B has PLE that Gemma 2 doesn't) plus WebGPU not yet exposing
cooperative_matrix / tensor cores.

## System prompt cache (demo/tests/build-cache.spec.ts + vite hook)

The 5500-token system prompt prefill takes 30-60s cold. Build pipeline
pre-computes the KV cache via playwright-driven headless Chrome and
bakes it into demo/public/system-cache.bin. Vite's buildStart hook
detects hash drift on main.ts + sdk-types.ts and regenerates
automatically — with VITE_SKIP_REGEN to break regen recursion,
stale-SingletonLock cleanup, webServer.timeout bump, and pkill sweep
so chrome instances can't leak across regens.

## Model loading (demo/src/draw/model-loader.ts)

OPFS-based GGUF fetch with streaming download, sidecar completion
marker, mid-stream failure recovery, stuck-writable fallback (chrome
createSyncAccessHandle → parallel file.slice), vision-tensor skip
(text-only workload, so ~150MB of vision weights stay off-GPU), and a
tensor-prefix survey log on startup for regression detection.

## Decode orchestration (demo/src/draw/main.ts + engine-worker.ts)

Two-phase streaming: thinking mode emits reasoning text into an
Excalidraw thinking cloud until <channel|> fires, then a reminder is
prefilled and the code phase streams into the editor + runs the
partial SDK executor on every statement. Orphan-node detection with
≥40% threshold retry; conversation-style retry with compile-error
feedback; KV-prefix reuse across submissions via rollbackKV; eager
prefill on keystroke (300ms debounce); grammar-constrained decoding
with S_IN_THINK pass-through and a 9-state JS-syntax state machine.

## SDK (demo/src/draw/drawmode/sdk.ts)

Deferred-linkup design: connect / addLane / addGroup / addFrame /
message record raw refs; resolveDeferred at render time resolves
them against the final node set, auto-infers lane membership from
connect edges (lane→box and box→lane both inherit), and propagates
lane membership through box→box chains. Empty lanes still render
as bands (positioned from neighbour lanes) so a swimlane-with-lanes-
not-connected looks like a swimlane instead of a flowchart.

## Cleanup

Removed all ORT-pivot artifacts from an earlier design iteration:
vendor/onnxruntime submodule, vendor/ort-shims, vendor/ort-patches,
scripts/build-wasm-llm.sh, scripts/fetch-ort-deps.sh,
scripts/export_gqa_model.py, src/tq_bridge.zig, src/inference/gguf.zig,
and the ort_lib + llm_exe build.zig targets that depended on them.
Nothing currently imports or builds against any of this.

src/tqstream.zig is retained — it backs the TQStream class in
src/js/index.ts that the npm package exports for streaming-use-case
vector encoding (unrelated to the ORT pivot).

## Impact on npm package

The existing src/*.zig core (polar, qjl, rotation, matrix, format,
math, turboquant, wasm_exports, tqstream) is unchanged from current
main behaviour — the npm package still builds and the existing
vector-search / image-similarity / 3DGS demos still use it. Only
the draw demo runs through the new WebGPU engine.

commit.message:
Revert "research: PLE compression feasibility study (pilot)"

This reverts commit 0ad1981529f1bb4091e62e7959f54a7d737a633c.

fix(demo): Firefox-aware error messaging

Previous error card pointed every non-Chrome user at 'switch to Chrome'
without explaining WHY. Firefox users reading it assumed the demo is
Chrome-exclusive forever, even though Firefox ships WebGPU on
Win/macOS.

Now we detect Firefox and explain the actual blocker: Firefox hasn't
shipped the WebGPU subgroups extension (`subgroupShuffleXor` and
friends), which the attention kernels depend on. Points at the
Mozilla tracking bug so readers can follow along.

Separate messages for: mobile (RAM cap), Firefox (subgroups missing),
generic no-WebGPU, adapter-request failure, adapter-missing-features.
Each says what's specifically missing.

research: PLE compression feasibility study (pilot)

Pilot toward DNA-style codebook compression for the 1.6 GB per-layer
embedding table in Gemma 4 E2B. Three scripts + findings:

01_extract_ple.py   extract per_layer_token_embd from the GGUF
                    (dequantizes Q5_K → f32 → casts to f16 for offline work)

02_analyze_structure.py   structural analysis
                          - per-layer std / mean / active %
                          - SVD on 50k vectors for global rank
                          - per-layer rank @ 95% energy
                          - cross-layer cosine similarity
                          - per-token variance across layers

03_train_pq.py      train Product Quantization codebooks at multiple
                    bit budgets, measure reconstruction MSE on a
                    200k-vector subsample

Findings (documented in task #117):

- PLE stores as Q5_K, 262144 vocab × (35 layers × 256 dim), 1614.8 MB
- Tight Gaussian distribution (std 0.064, range ±1.1)
- Every layer full-rank (rank_95 ≈ 237/256 dims)
- Cross-layer cosine ≈ 0 — each (token, layer) vector is independent
- SVD / low-rank / cross-layer factoring all ruled out

PQ empirical results at 200k sample:
  config         bits/vec  size   ratio   rel_MSE
  PQ-32×256       256      294 MB  5.5×   0.32
  PQ-16×256       128      147 MB  11×    0.56
  PQ-16×16         64       73 MB  22×    0.78
  PQ-4×4096        48       59 MB  27×    0.75

Rel MSE 0.3+ is concerning — Q5_K's rel MSE is ~0.001. PQ alone can't
hit DNA-tier (30×) compression for PLE with acceptable quality.

Next steps (if pursuing further):
- End-to-end test with PQ-32×256 in the engine to measure actual
  downstream quality (PLE may tolerate high MSE as a residual stream)
- Residual VQ layered on top of PQ to halve MSE per level
- Budget for pivot back to no-compression if empirical test regresses

fix(demo): auto-strip undeclared refs + bigger class example

Two fixes for common small-model failure modes observed yesterday:

1. Class test kept failing with 'PayPal is not defined' because the
   2-subclass example (dog, cat) didn't give the model enough pattern
   to extrapolate to 4-subclass prompts. Rewrote the class.ts example
   to an abstract Shape + 4 concrete subclasses (Circle/Square/
   Triangle/Rectangle) with 4 inheritance edges, so 'abstract base
   with N subtypes' prompts have a 1:1 structural match. Also added
   explicit 'connect arg order is (from, to, label, opts)' rule after
   the model mis-ordered to (from, label, to, opts).

2. Architecture 'how apple make money' test emitted
   addGroup('Hardware', [appleHardware, MacBook, watchOS]) where
   MacBook was undeclared (actual var was const macBook — case drift)
   and connect(appleHardware, revenue, ...) where revenue was a
   hallucinated identifier. Runtime pre-execute pass
   stripUndeclaredReferences now drops any connect / message /
   addGroup / addLane line whose positional arg-identifiers aren't
   in the set of addXxx-returned consts. Other diagram still renders;
   the one broken edge / group-member is dropped rather than
   triggering a full retry cycle.

Cache rebuilt (hash 795c0d0a822c5723).

fix(demo): keep editor non-empty on retry failure + enforce const on every addClass

- main.ts tracks bestAttemptCode (the longest code any attempt
  produced during the run). After the attempt loop exits with all
  attempts failed, if the editor contents are shorter than the best
  attempt (e.g. attempt 1 produced 689ch with one fixable typo,
  attempt 2 produced 18ch and wiped the editor) we restore the best
  one so the user has the most context to hand-edit.

- class.ts: add explicit rule that EVERY addClass call must be
  assigned to a const. The previous run showed the model declaring
  Payment + CreditCard with const + connecting them, then switching
  to bare 'PayPal = addClass(...)' from the 3rd class onward,
  triggering 'PayPal is not defined' at the first connect. Sited
  clearly at rule #1.

fix(demo): retry wiring + class brace-matching + sequence orphan-actor rule

Three follow-ups from yesterday's interactive testing:

1. Retry loop was declaring lastMountedBranch but never assigning it
   after a successful mount. Result: retry on attempt 2 fell back to
   router, the model (seeing its own setType in conversation history)
   declined to re-emit it, runCodePhaseOnly silently discarded every
   token, empty output. Fixed by setting lastMountedBranch = mountTarget
   after the post-mount runThinkingAndCode completes. Retries now
   correctly skip router and remount the previously-picked branch.

2. class branch produced stray '}' on attempt 1 (same brace-matching
   class as ER). Tightened class.ts with an explicit brace-counting
   rule: 'After methods ']', next chars are '});' — not '},});' or
   ',});'.

3. sequence branch declared actors (e.g. a 'User' actor for OAuth
   flows) without any message referencing them, leaving orphan columns.
   Rule was buried deep in the prompt; added it as the FIRST rule and
   changed the example so User kicks off the flow ('User clicks login'
   → 'Browser POST /login'). Model pattern-matches on examples more
   than rules, so the example change is load-bearing.

Cache rebuilt (96.6 MB TQKC, 9 branches, hash c53d67a1bc6ca86b).

fix(demo): skip router on retry + tighten ER/orgchart prompts + strip vite auto-regen

Three symptoms from interactive testing last session:

1. ER prompt produced stray '}' in attempt 1 (lost brace-matching
   mid-table); attempts 2+3 ran under router and silently emitted 0
   tokens because the model didn't re-emit setType on retry — the
   router observer discards everything without a setType fire.
2. Orgchart interleaved connect() among addBox() declarations and hit
   'not defined' errors on attempt 1; worked on attempt 2 only because
   the model happened to re-emit setType.
3. bun run rebuild-cache failed when vite's configureServer fired its
   own regen on top of the already-running rebuild — recursion killed
   the outer test's chrome via SingletonLock cleanup.

Fixes:
- main.ts: track lastMountedBranch across attempts. On retry, skip
  router entirely and mount the previous attempt's branch directly.
  Retries now get full think+code under the right specialised KV.
- prompts/preamble.ts: add rule 'declare every node before any
  connect/edge that references it; safe order is nodes first, then
  edges'. Applies to all branches.
- prompts/orgchart.ts: reiterate the declare-before-use rule.
- prompts/er.ts: tighten column-shape spec, add explicit 'exactly
  one closing brace per opening, addTable closes with ])', grow the
  example to 6 tables matching the common e-commerce prompt so the
  model has a closer template.
- vite.config.ts: remove configureServer's auto-regen and all
  supporting infra (checkAndRegen / regenerateCache /
  cleanupPlaywrightChromes / POST /api/write-system-cache). No auto-
  regen anywhere — cache is the committed source of truth; run
  bun run rebuild-cache explicitly on a WebGPU dev box when prompts
  change.

Cache regenerated: 95.9 MB TQKC, 9 branches. bun x tsc clean,
vite build clean in 12s.

fix(demo): vite build never auto-regens cache

The buildStart hook was spawning playwright to regenerate the cache
mid-build. Playwright needs WebGPU, which GitHub Actions runners
don't have, so the Pages deploy hung for 10+ min before timing out.

vite build now just bundles the committed public/system-cache.bin —
no playwright, no WebGPU, no prompt-hash check. If the cache is
stale, bun run rebuild-cache on a local dev machine produces a new
cache + hash to commit.

Also extends promptSourceHash in vite.config.ts + scripts/write-prompt-hash.mjs
to include all 9 prompt files (was 6 — missing flowchart/state/orgchart/er/class/swimlane)
so the dev-server regen path picks up edits to any branch. Hash
file written with the new 12-file formula.

feat(demo): grammar-triggered KV branch swap on setType (#9)

Dynamic context window for the Gemma 4 E2B draw demo. Engine boots with a small "router" system prompt; when the model emits setType("..."), a side-channel observer detects it and engine.mountKV swaps the active KV to that type's pre-baked branch — no runtime prefill on the swap.

9 per-type branches under demo/src/draw/prompts/ (router + architecture / sequence / flowchart / state / orgchart / er / class / swimlane), packed into a new TQKC multi-branch container format at public/system-cache.bin (88.3 MB). Design: demo/docs/dynamic-context-window.md.

Core pieces:
- ModeTracker in grammar.ts with 9 SDK_MODE constants + onEnter hooks
- engine.mountKV(name) + registerBranch
- worker mountBranchAndPrefill: swap KV + re-prefill user turn atomically
- DiagramType union expanded to all 8 values in types.ts + sdk-types.ts
- Router skips the thinking phase (its only job is to pick setType); branch runs full think+code under specialised KV
- Retry restores to router snapshot (activeBranch bookkeeping included)

Rolls in demo improvements accumulated on this branch: subgroup-butterfly reductions, window-bound softmax, shared-KV skip in batched prefill, uniform-buffer dedup, mobile-aware WebGPU error card, markdown-stripped thinking cloud, cancel-and-restart, singleton lock, orphan auto-fix before retry, retry-LCP rollback.

Follow-up to #8: validatePayloadSizes now delegates to polarBytesNeeded / qjlBytesNeeded instead of re-deriving the formulas, plus symmetric qjl_bytes-corruption tests. Package bumped to 0.4.1.

22 new TS unit tests + 2 new Zig tests (63 total), all pass. TS clean, zig fmt clean, CI green across Linux/macOS/Windows.

commit.message: embed_lookup_int4 kernel — ONNX GatherBlockQuantized semantics
onnx reader — protobuf decoder + inline/external tensor support

kernels: asymmetric uint4 (ONNX MatMulNBits-compatible)

Step 1 of the ONNX-native refactor. The Zig int4 kernels now decode
`(uint4 - zp) * scale` per block, matching ONNX's MatMulNBits layout.
Both paths converge on one format — the existing blob still works via
our converter, and the forthcoming ONNX loader will produce byte-
identical input.

Kernel changes (`src/zig/wasm_exports.zig`):
- `int4BlockDecode(row, block_d, zp: u8)` — subtract zero-point in
  i16 space before widening to f32. Now takes zp per call.
- `extractZp(zp_row, b)` helper — unpacks the per-block uint4 zp
  from a `[N, ceil(nblocks/2)]` byte buffer.
- `matmul_bf16_x_int4block`, `matmul_bf16_x_int4block_out_f32`,
  `matmul_f32_x_int4block_out_f32` — new `w_zp_ptr: ?[*]const u8`
  parameter. Pass `0` for symmetric-equivalent decode (zp=0).

Converter (`scripts/convert_weights.py`):
- `quantize_int4_block32_sym` now emits asymmetric uint4 blockwise
  with per-block zero-points. Scale = (max-min)/15; zp = round(-min/scale).
  Packed layout: int4 [N, D/2] + fp16 scales [N, D/32] + uint4 zp
  [N, ceil(D/32/2)], back-to-back. The function name is kept for
  call-site stability (will disappear when we drop the custom blob).

Fixture + runner wiring:
- `scripts/gen-kernel-fixtures.py::dequantize_int4block` + 3D expert
  dequant updated to read the new zp region.
- `scripts/verify-kernels.mjs::runMatmulInt4` passes zp pointer.
- `src/js/inference/expert.ts::expertSlicePointers` computes per-
  expert zp offset and threads it into the matmul calls.

All 14 kernel fixtures green. `expert_dispatch` tolerance was 3.9e-3
(symmetric) → 3.1e-2 abs (asymmetric); still well inside tol.
model_forward maxRel 0.84 → 0.24 — the extra ZP degree of freedom
gives the quantizer better accuracy overall.

Node e2e, Chromium smoke + public-api all pass.

Next units: ONNX reader in TS, embed_lookup_int4, swap WasmBackend
to consume `onnx/model_q4f16.onnx` directly.

inline pii.wasm into the JS bundle

The 23 KB pii.wasm is baked into the published library as a Uint8Array
literal, removing one HTTP round trip during first-call setup and
sidestepping the `new URL(..., import.meta.url)` path-resolution issue
in bundled output.

- New `scripts/inline-wasm.mjs` runs as part of `npm run build`, reads
  `dist/pii.wasm`, emits `src/js/backends/pii-wasm-inline.ts` with the
  bytes as an array literal.
- `loadPiiWasm(url?)` now falls back to those inline bytes when no URL
  is given; `WasmBackend.warmup()` defaults to that path.
- `CreateOptions.wasmModuleUrl` / `WasmBackendOptions.wasmModuleUrl` stay
  as opt-in overrides for users hosting a different .wasm build.

Bundle size: 1.1 MB → 1.28 MB (180 KB for the hex-encoded 23 KB wasm;
gzip will compress it back down). Public-API smoke test in Chromium
dropped from 1.42 s → 1.22 s create time.

Also updates docs/benchmarks.md with the honest cold-start accounting
— transformers.js and WasmBackend fetch comparable weight payloads
(770 MB ONNX vs 944 MB blob); prior "2.96 s vs 15.7 s warmup" was
apples-to-oranges (loopback vs HF Hub). On parity terms, first-call
differs by ~1-3 s (WebGPU pipeline compile savings), not 13 s.

stage 1 phase G: browser perf bench vs transformers.js WebGPU

Adds a playwright bench that loads both PrivacyFilter backends in one
Chromium page and measures warm-forward medians across T=8/25/61:

- transformers.js (default): downloads ONNX from HF Hub, auto-selects
  WebGPU when available (confirmed enabled via `--enable-unsafe-webgpu`
  + `--use-angle=metal` in playwright.config.ts).
- WasmBackend: loads the full 944 MB blob via a gitignored symlink
  to `/tmp/pii-wasm-e2e/pii-weights-full.bin` (produced by
  gen-full-parity-fixture.py).

Not a CI test — the full blob is not committed. Invoke locally via
`bun x playwright test tests/browser/bench.spec.ts`.

stage 1 phase F: browser tests + TR-tiled bf16 matmul

Two orthogonal changes that both land behind the same "fully working
and faster" umbrella from the follow-up pass.

Browser tests (fully working):
- playwright.config.ts with a python -m http.server webServer.
- tests/browser/smoke.spec.ts: WasmBackend direct-call parity against
  the committed model_forward fixture (argmax 16/16 in Chromium,
  warmup 39 ms, forward 13 ms on the 1-layer truncated blob).
- tests/browser/public-api.spec.ts: PrivacyFilter.create({ backend:
  "wasm" }) end-to-end — tokenizer download + viterbi + wasm backend,
  returns a valid RedactResult.
- WasmBackend + CreateOptions now take `wasmModuleUrl` so the bundled
  JS can point at a non-sibling .wasm. Bun's bundler preserves the
  `new URL(..., import.meta.url)` literal, which doesn't resolve in
  browser hosting; this option lets callers pin it explicitly.
- `WasmBackend` exposed as a named export (advanced API, for tests
  and power users who want to skip tokenizer/chunking).

Perf (make it fast):
- matmul_bf16 gets TR=4 outer tiling over T, amortizing the W-row
  load across 4 X rows. 8 accumulator vectors (2 per T row) expose
  enough ILP for V8's SIMD backend to schedule dual mul+add pairs.
  **~23 GFLOPS** on T≥16 shapes (up from ~17), dropping to ~8 GFLOPS
  on T=8/N=33 where tile setup dominates (absolute 40 µs → negligible).
- Full-model e2e bench: 4–5% total speedup; the win is bounded by
  the MoE path dominating (int4 matmul, ~60% of bf16 peak).
- `@mulAdd` on @Vector(4, f32) was tried and reverted — Zig's
  wasm-freestanding codegen falls back to scalar software fma, running
  ~6× slower than the separate-mul-add vector form. Documented in the
  matmul comment for the next person.

docs/benchmarks.md updated with current numbers + browser smoke.

stage 1 phase E: warmup, public API, mask, full parity, xbackend, bench

Closes the 6 remaining items from the phase-D review pass.

- Warmup prefill: WasmBackend.warmup now runs one dummy forward at
  T=16 so V8 JITs every hot-path kernel and the bump heap hits its
  steady-state size before the user's first real request.

- Public API wiring: CreateOptions.backend accepts "wasm" plus
  `wasmWeightsUrl` + `wasmWeightsSha256`. privacy-filter.ts threads
  them through to selectBackend; browser prefers WebGPU for the
  transformers.js path but obeys the explicit "wasm" override.

- Padding mask: banded_attention gains an optional `mask_ptr: ?*u8`
  (NULL = all valid). Invalid keys get -∞ logit so softmax assigns
  them zero probability. Plumbed through attentionForward →
  blockForward* → modelForward → WasmBackend.forward. The backend
  skips the alloc/copy when every mask byte is 1. New fixture
  `banded_attention` with padding mask passes at rel_tol 2e-2.

- Full-model parity: `gen-full-parity-fixture.py` builds the full
  944 MB blob (all 128 experts, int4 for MoE, bf16 elsewhere) and a
  PyTorch reference; `verify-full-parity.mjs` runs it through
  WasmBackend. On a 41-token sample: argmax class agreement 41/41,
  logit rms 0.33 — KPI is bit-equivalent classification. Not a CI
  test (fixture too big to commit).

- Cross-backend parity: `verify-xbackend-parity.mjs` compares
  WasmBackend vs TransformersJsBackend on identical inputs via the
  shared Tokenizer. 41/41 argmax agreement.

- E2E bench: `bench-e2e.mjs` reports warm-forward medians across
  T=8/25/61. docs/benchmarks.md updated with the Node CPU numbers
  plus the apples-to-oranges caveat (onnxruntime-node uses native
  BLAS + threading; our Zig+WASM is single-threaded SIMD128; the
  relevant comparison is ORT Web's WASM fallback in a browser,
  which requires a browser harness we haven't built yet).

Phase E is DONE for the shippable-in-Node story. Remaining work to
declare Stage 1 fully shipped: browser playwright bench against ORT
Web's WASM fallback.

stage 1 phase D.11: WasmBackend e2e parity + selectBackend wiring

- WasmBackend.forward actually works now: loads .wasm, loads
  pii-weights.bin, tokenizes its input via the caller, runs
  modelForward, upcasts bf16 logits → f32 for the InferenceBackend
  contract. Auto-detects num_layers + num_experts from the blob so
  it works with both the truncated test blob and a full production
  blob. PiiWasmExports exported so the inference/ TS modules can
  reference it.
- selectBackend({ backend: "wasm", wasmWeightsUrl }) now dispatches
  to WasmBackend. Omitting the backend param keeps the default
  transformers.js path.
- convert_weights.py --expert-keep N now also truncates the router
  weight + bias along dim 0. Previously only `mlp.experts.*` were
  truncated — the router stayed at [128, 640] which produced
  indices > N that the truncated expert blob couldn't serve. Caught
  by the WasmBackend e2e test.
- scripts/verify-backend-e2e.mjs: end-to-end parity against the
  committed 1-layer-truncated blob. Same numbers as the standalone
  model_forward kernel test (rms 0.014, maxAbs 0.06 on logits),
  confirming the backend's warmup + forward path is equivalent.
  npm run verify:e2e.

Phase D done. Remaining work is full-model weight blob generation
on demand + transformers.js-specific parity (browser) for Phase E.

stage 1 phase D.10: block + model forward composition

- src/js/inference/block.ts:
  - routerForward: matmul_bf16_out_f32 + topk + softmax + /K
  - blockForwardWithRouting: norm + attn + residual + norm + experts
    + residual, takes routing from caller (for tests with truncated
    experts)
  - blockForward: full block with self-contained router
- src/js/inference/model.ts:
  - modelForward: embed + N blocks (ping-pong buffers) + final norm +
    classifier. One rope table precompute reused across blocks.
- New kernel `add_bf16`: bf16 + bf16 → bf16 for residual connections.
  Matches PyTorch's eager bf16 arithmetic (one rounding).
- Converter `--quant-transpose` pattern matching switched to endswith
  so mlp.experts.gate_up_proj doesn't accidentally trigger on the
  gate_up_proj_bias sibling.
- PiiWasmExports exported so downstream JS modules (attention.ts,
  expert.ts, block.ts, model.ts) can name the type.

Fixtures (truncated to 4 experts per layer):
- block_forward: random hidden → upstream block-with-patched-experts
  reference. rel_tol 15% / abs_tol 1.2 accommodates compound drift
  across 4 bf16 matmul chains + attn + MoE; first-5 output values
  match within 1% in practice (rms 0.24).
- model_forward: 1-layer-truncated model, random input_ids → full
  forward → logits. rel_tol 20% / abs_tol 1.5 — actual measured
  rms 0.014, maxAbs 0.06; the final classifier head doesn't amplify
  drift much, so model-level parity is tighter than block-level.

stage 1 phase D.9: MoE expert dispatch (expert-major)

- Two new f32-output int4 matmul variants:
    matmul_bf16_x_int4block_out_f32  (gate_up: bf16 x → int4 W → f32)
    matmul_f32_x_int4block_out_f32   (down: f32 x → int4 W → f32)
  Factored into `matmulInt4BlockImpl` with comptime XType/OutType.
- Signature change: int4 matmul now takes separate `w_int4_ptr` and
  `w_scales_ptr`. Needed because per-expert slicing of a 3D int4 tensor
  puts scales at a different offset than `int4 + N*D/2`.
- Helper kernels for scatter gather/scatter flow:
    gather_bf16                — dst[i,:] = src[indices[i],:]
    scatter_add_weighted_f32   — target[indices[i]] += w[i] * values[i]
    zero_f32                   — bump alloc returns uninitialized
    cast_f32_to_bf16_scaled    — final * num_experts_per_tok + bf16
- Converter extensions:
    --quant-transpose PATTERN  — swap last two dims before quantizing
                                 (upstream MoE weights are [E,D_in,D_out];
                                 our matmul wants [E,D_out,D_in])
    --expert-keep N            — truncate 3D mlp.experts.* to first N
  Pattern match switched to `endswith` so `gate_up_proj` doesn't
  accidentally pick up `gate_up_proj_bias`.
- src/js/inference/expert.ts: expert-major dispatch. Inverts routing,
  batches tokens per expert, runs the int4/f32 chain, scatter-adds
  weighted into an f32 accumulator, scales by num_experts_per_tok,
  downcasts to bf16.
- Fixture: first-4 experts of layer 0, synthetic uniform routing. maxRel
  7e-3 vs a reference that dequantizes our blob's weights (so the test
  bounds kernel rounding only, not quant error).

stage 1 phase D.8: attention forward composition

- src/js/inference/attention.ts: wires Q/K/V projections + RoPE +
  head_dim^-0.25 scale + banded attention + O projection into one
  call. Uses the bump allocator for Q/K/V/attn_out scratch.
- New kernels:
  - scale_bf16_inplace: y[i] = round_bf16(bf16(y[i]) * scale). Used
    for the Q/K post-RoPE scaling. Matches upstream's one-rounding
    multiply exactly.
  - banded_attention sinks_ptr changed from u16 to *f32 — upstream's
    _keep_in_fp32_modules_strict keeps sinks in fp32 even in a bf16
    model.
- Fixture: layer-0 weights from openai/privacy-filter loaded into the
  test blob (5 → 11 tensors, 1.3 → 3.8 MB). hidden [T=32, D=640] bf16
  random input, expected output computed via upstream's real
  self_attn submodule.
- Tolerance 5% rel / 5% abs. maxAbs 0.25, rms 0.01 — cumulative drift
  across 4 matmul + rope + attention + scale + sink chain.

Related fixes caught in runner integration:
- verify-kernels.mjs weight map now exposes both `.ptr`+`.dataOffset`
  so JS callers and WeightTensorInfo consumers see consistent fields.

stage 1 phase D.7: banded attention (scores + combine + sinks)

GQA attention kernel with sliding window. Computes only the in-window
cells (O(T * window) not O(T^2)); window * 2 + 1 keys max per query.
Sink column folded into the softmax denominator before AV combine, so
probability mass can shift to the sink without writing a separate slot.

Kernel structure:
- dotBf16F32 helper: 4-wide vector dot product with 4 independent f32
  accumulators, same pattern as the matmul inner loop. Amortized for
  head_dim=64.
- saxpyF32Bf16 helper: p * v + acc, vector over head_dim.
- Three passes per (t_q, h_q): scores, softmax exp+sum, weighted-V sum.
  No intermediate [T,T] materialization.

Fixture: T=32, H_q=14 (GQA 7:1), head_dim=64, window=4, random Q/K/V
(Q/K pre-scaled by head_dim^-0.25 per upstream convention). maxRel 5.4e-3
vs end-to-end PyTorch reference (tolerance 2e-2 — loose to accommodate
the 3-rounding bf16 chain through QK + AV + exp + normalize).

stage 1 phase D.6: router primitives — matmul_bf16_out_f32 + topk

Router runs in fp32 upstream; exposing an f32-output matmul keeps
the precision intact across the router projection. topk_partial_f32
does per-row top-k via a fixed-size incremental insertion sort (k=4,
n=128 → 512 compares per row, beats a heap for small k).

Bench fixtures cover both ends:
- matmul_bf16_out_f32: maxRel 3.89e-5 (tol 1e-3). Same matrix as the
  bf16 test; only the final bf16 round is skipped.
- topk_partial_f32: bit-exact indices + values across 32 rows × 4 top
  entries vs torch.topk.

TS-level router composition (matmul → topk → softmax → /k) lands with
the block forward wiring.

stage 1 phase D.5: swiglu_clamp_f32 kernel

Matches OpenAIPrivacyFilterExperts._apply_gate exactly:
  gate = clamp(gate, max=7); up = clamp(up, -7, 7)
  glu = gate * sigmoid(gate * 1.702)
  out = (up + 1) * glu

f32 throughout — the upstream expert forward upcasts to fp32 for the
full MLP chain. sigmoid uses the numerically-stable branch
(exp argument kept ≤ 0).

Tested on [T=16, D=640] with input spread to hit both clamp branches:
maxRel 2.92e-7, well inside the 1e-5 tolerance.

stage 1 phase D.4: softmax_f32 kernel

Row-wise f32 softmax with max-subtract for numerical stability. Three
passes: max, exp+sum, normalize. Scalar exp per lane — WASM has no
vector exp; Zig's @exp uses its freestanding software exp.

Tested against PyTorch F.softmax on two shapes:
- attention: [8, 257] (8 queries × window 128+128+1 sink)
- topk: [16, 4] (router top-4)

Both within 1e-5 relative / 1e-7 absolute. maxRel ~5e-7 on attention
shape, ~2e-7 on topk — well inside the tolerance.
clean up stale f32 rope fixtures (bf16 supersedes)

stage 1 phase D.3: RoPE yarn — precompute + apply

- src/js/inference/rope.ts: YARN inv_freq computation (ramp between
  extrapolation and interpolation inv_freqs using beta_fast/beta_slow
  correction range). attention_scaling = 0.1*log(factor)+1. cos/sin
  tables emitted as bf16 (matches PyTorch's internal downcast to the
  caller's dtype).
- rope_apply kernel: in-place rotation on [T, H, head_dim] bf16 with
  interleaved pair layout. Uses PyTorch's eager 3-rounding bf16
  semantics to match the reference bit-exactly (upcast → mul → round
  → upcast → combine → round). Noisier than a single-rounding f32
  chain but bit-identical to upstream.
- verify-rope-precompute.mjs: JS rope table computation tested against
  bf16 fixture from PyTorch — 256/256 bit-exact each for cos + sin.
- rope_apply tested via the main kernel verifier — 7168/7168 bit-exact
  against PyTorch _apply_rotary_emb on T=8 / H=14 / head_dim=64.
bench-kernels: add int4 shape coverage

stage 1 phase D.2: matmul optimization stack

- matmul_bf16: 4 independent @Vector(4, f32) accumulators unrolled
  16-wide on D, horizontal-reduce at the end. ~17 GFLOPS (up from
  2.7). Still 264/264 bit-exact vs PyTorch F.linear at bf16 output.
- matmul_bf16_x_int4block: wide 16-byte int4 load per block with
  sign-extended nibble unpack, 8 compile-time shuffle masks to carve
  4-lane slices. TR=4 outer tiling across T amortizes int4 decode
  across 4 rows sharing a W row. ~13 GFLOPS (up from 3.3). Still
  bit-exact at bf16 output vs F.linear(x, dequant(W), b).

GPU advantage for this model is ~3x over CPU, not the 30-100x seen with

wire webgpu backend into the public API

selectBackend routes backend:"webgpu" to WebGpuBackend; CreateOptions
exposes it in the types. privacy-filter.ts threads the choice through
and reports the right stage name in the compile progress event.

webgpu-smoke.spec.ts is the cross-backend equivalent of smoke.spec.ts:
PrivacyFilter.create({backend:"webgpu"}) vs the transformers.js
baseline on a representative English sentence. Expected output: 2
spans ("John Smith" / "john@example.com"), byte-identical between
backends. Argmax parity from webgpu-forward.spec.ts implies this, but
exercising the full tokenizer + Viterbi + BIOES decode catches any
off-by-one in the public-API wiring.

webgpu: align storage-buffer binding sizes + full-forward parity test

The ONNX score.zp buffer is 330 bytes — a non-multiple of 4 that
WebGPU validation rejects on read-only-storage bindings. Padding
resBinding's size to the next 4-byte boundary fixes the validation
error that was silently discarding every dispatch in the forward
pass. The matmulInt4Test helper now goes through the same resBinding
path for the same reason.

Also surface uncaptured WebGPU errors via console.error so future
binding mismatches are visible to test harnesses.

webgpu-forward.spec.ts runs one token sequence through both backends
and asserts per-token argmax agreement + loose maxAbs/rms bounds on
the logits. Argmax is the downstream-meaningful signal for Viterbi
and span decode; the looser magnitude caps accommodate fp16 drift
compounding over 8 layers (GPU native f16 vs WASM fp16↔f32 emul).

On M-series + Chromium: argmax 32/32, maxAbs=1.81, rms=0.18,
GPU forward ~240 ms, WASM forward ~240 ms (single-batch).

stage 2 phase B: full forward on WebGPU

Ports the WASM forward onto WGSL kernels. embed_lookup_int4, rms_norm,
matmul_int4 (f32→f32 and f32→f16), rope_apply, banded_attention,
router_topk, swiglu_clamp, qmoe gate_up + down_scatter, add_fp16,
casts, and zero. Warmup compiles all 14 pipelines in parallel.

MoE dispatch is token-major: one workgroup per (token, k_pick) in
two passes — gate_up+swiglu writes the glu row into a scratch
buffer, then down_proj atomically scatter-adds weight × out into
the per-token accumulator via atomicCompareExchangeWeak on f32 bits.
Avoids the CPU readback the expert-major WASM path needs.

RoPE uses native WGSL f16 arithmetic so `a*c - b*s` rounds three
times per output, matching PyTorch eager fp16. RMSNorm reduces
sum-of-squares across a 64-thread workgroup via tree reduction.
Banded attention holds scores + softmax in workgroup memory, with
one thread per head_dim lane for the AV combine.

Existing matmul_int4 parity test still passes unchanged. Full
forward parity vs WASM lands in a follow-up once the model weights
are warm on this machine.

stage 2 phase A: WebGPU backend scaffolding + matmul_int4 WGSL kernel

Lays down the Stage 2 path. `WebGpuBackend` requests a device with
the `shader-f16` feature, uploads every weight tensor from the
shared ONNX graph into persistent GPU storage buffers (same byte
layout the WASM path uses — int4 + f16 scales + packed-uint4 zp +
f16 bias; router scales down-converted f32→f16 at load), and
compiles one compute pipeline so far: `matmul_int4` (f32 x × int4
block W → f32 out).

The shader dequants in-loop and fma-accumulates per-block in f32;
one thread per (t, n) output, 64-thread workgroups. It's the hot
kernel but not the final form — we'll tile for workgroup-shared
X later. Remaining kernels (rms_norm, rope, banded_attention,
swiglu, router_topk, qmoe, embed_lookup_int4, cast_f32→fp16 etc.)
land next; `forward()` still throws.

Parity test: browser-run matmul on real layers.0.attn.q_proj weights
vs the WASM `matmul_f32_x_int4block_out_f32` on identical input —
maxAbs 1.2e-3, rms 5.3e-5, well inside fp16 tolerance. Single
dispatch is slower than WASM (~36 ms vs 0.4 ms) because it eats
first-use pipeline compile + pipe overhead; real gain comes when
the full forward keeps intermediates on the GPU.

Adds `@webgpu/types` dev-dep + tsconfig types entry; exposes
`WebGpuBackend` and `ModelLoader` from the public index so tests
can drive them directly.

perf: fold matmul bias add into the sum vector (one SIMD add, not 8 scalar)

Instead of adding bias per-lane as the output stores, splat the fp16
bias into a @Vector(8, f32) and do one SIMD add to the tile's sum
vector. Saves 7 scalar adds per N output.

Browser bench: 66/159/346 → 64/154/333 ms (~2% faster).
Parity: cross-backend smoke 2/2.

perf: SIMD approximate exp for SwiGLU sigmoid + attention softmax

Adds a 4-lane minimax polynomial approximation for non-positive
exponents (≤0), accurate to ~3×10⁻⁷ relative. Drops into the two
exp hot spots: SwiGLU's sigmoid (via the negate-and-branch identity)
and the banded-attention softmax (inputs are already `score − max`).

Browser bench: 65/157/341 → 65/156/336 ms. Cross-backend span parity
unchanged (smoke 2/2).

perf: more aggressive wasm-opt inlining

Pass --inlining-optimizing plus larger always-inline / flexible-inline
thresholds (256 / 512 bytes) and --inline-functions-with-loops. Shakes
out a few more cross-function call sites in the hot path.

Browser bench: 66/159/346 → 65/157/341 ms (~1–2% faster).
wasm size up modestly; still inlined into the JS bundle.

perf: FMA in scatter_add + banded_attention dot/saxpy

Small cleanup: the remaining mul+add pairs in scatter_add_weighted_f32
and banded_attention's dotBf16F32 / saxpyF32Bf16 didn't pick up
relaxed_madd from just the earlier int4-matmul change. Route them
through the same fma4 helper.

Browser bench: 67/164/358 → 66/159/346 ms (~2% faster). Cross-backend
span parity holds.

perf: vectorise swiglu clamp/gate over SIMD lanes

4-at-a-time loop over the interleaved (gate, up) pairs: shuffle-
deinterleave, SIMD clamp + scale, scalar-per-lane sigmoid (wasm has
no SIMD exp), SIMD mul for the gated output. Scalar tail retained.

Browser bench barely moves (exp is the cost, not the arithmetic),
but the kernel is cleaner and the scaffolding is there for a later
approximate-exp swap.

Parity: cross-backend smoke still 2/2 spans agree.

perf: wire f32x4.relaxed_madd into int4 matmul MAC loop (FMA)

Zig's @mulAdd(@Vector(4, f32), ...) falls back to scalar per-lane
software FMA on wasm, defeating relaxed-SIMD. Clang's
__builtin_wasm_relaxed_madd_f32x4 emits the opcode directly, so add
a tiny C helper (src/c/fma.c, always_inline) and link-time-inline
it into the Zig matmul via `-flto` on both sides.

288 f32x4.relaxed_madd ops now live in dist/pii.wasm; every MAC in
the int4 matmul hot path is fused (was mul + add).

  short  71 → 66 ms   (0.40× → 0.46× vs tjs)
  medium 172 → 160 ms (0.22× → 0.24×)
  long   374 → 347 ms (0.15× → 0.16×)

Cumulative since the ONNX pivot: 211/579/1341 → 66/160/347 ms
(3.2–3.9× faster). Requires a runtime with `relaxed_simd` (V8 yes,
Node ≥ 21 yes, Bun not yet).

public-api.spec.ts green.

`normed2` (post-attn RMSNorm) is consumed by both the router matmul
and every expert's gate_up. Widen it to f32 once inside blockForward
and pass the f32 buffer to routerForward + expertDispatch. Removes the
router's fp16-input matmul (last non-f32 int4 kernel call on the hot
path) and the per-expert fp16→f32 convert step.

New kernel `gather_f32` mirrors `gather_fp16` so experts can pull
their token rows straight out of the f32 buffer.

Browser bench:
  short  72 → 71 ms   (0.42× → 0.40× vs tjs)

perf: pre-convert attn + classifier x to f32 once per matmul

Same trick that paid off for experts: widen x from fp16 to f32 once
before every attention Q/K/V/O projection (and the classifier head)

  long   530 → 383 ms  (0.11× → 0.15×)

perf: pre-convert expert x to f32 once, drop per-load fp16 widen

The fp16-input int4 matmul widens each x load from fp16 to f32
inside the inner MAC loop. For the MoE gate_up hot path every x
column is read ~N times per call, so the widening work compounds.

New kernel `convert_fp16_to_f32` bulk-widens a contiguous buffer;
expert dispatch gathers the expert's tokens into fp16, widens once
into an f32 scratch, then calls the existing f32-input int4 matmul.

  medium 390 → 237 ms  (0.10× → 0.16×)
  long   902 → 530 ms  (0.06× → 0.11×)

perf: inline fp16↔f32 converters as integer bit-ops

Zig's @floatCast(f16 ↔ f32) lowers to a software libcall on wasm
(LLVM has no native f16 ABI), so both conversions lived as standalone
wasm functions and got called once per scale, bias, and stored
activation — roughly 21 calls per output row in the int4 matmuls.

Rewrote both as explicit integer bit manipulations with the same
semantics as the upstream f16 cast. LLVM inlines these into the hot
loops. Function-call sites in dist/pii.wasm: 106 → 10.

Browser bench:
  short  156 → 141 ms  (0.19× → 0.21× vs tjs)
  medium 420 → 390 ms  (0.09× → 0.10×)
  long   958 → 902 ms  (0.06× → 0.06×)

Parity unchanged: 8/8 argmax, max logit diff 0.97.

perf: int4 matmul TR=4 → TR=8, 5–10% more end-to-end

Bigger T tile amortises one int4-block decode across 8 tokens (was 4),
halving the decode:MAC ratio in the attention/classifier/expert
matmul hot path. Dropped the 2-way ILP accumulator on each T row and
held the per-tile sums in a @Vector(8, f32) for the cross-block
accumulation — stays inside wasm's 16 v128 register budget.

Node profile (T=61): 972 → 882 ms forward.

  medium 435 → 420 ms (0.09× → 0.09×)
  long  1011 → 958 ms (0.06× → 0.06×)

Cumulative since the ONNX pivot: 211/579/1341 → 156/420/958 ms
(~28% faster across the board). Parity unchanged: 8/8 argmax.

perf: 25% faster forward via leaner fp16 widen + amortized matmul tail

Two changes to the int4 matmul hot path, driven by a profile of a
T=61 forward:
- fp16x4ToF32x4 rewritten as 7-op integer sequence (down from ~15);
  uses the `sign | (bits<<13) + (112<<23)` identity for finite
  non-zeros, masked off for true zero.
- MatMul tail (T % 4 != 0) now amortises the int4 block decode
  across the 1..3 remaining tokens instead of re-decoding per token.
  Hot for expert dispatch where m rarely hits 4.

Node profile (T=61): 1234 ms → 972 ms total forward (-21%).
Browser bench (WebGPU tjs vs our wasm):
  short  211 → 158 ms  (0.16× → 0.20× vs tjs)
  medium 579 → 435 ms  (0.07× → 0.09×)
  long  1341 → 1011 ms (0.04× → 0.06×)

Parity unchanged: 8/8 argmax, max logit diff 0.97. Zig tests 9/9.

stage 1 phase G: ONNX pivot + 8/8 parity with transformers.js

Replaces the custom pii-weights.bin blob with direct reads of upstream
onnx/model_q4f16.onnx — same 772 MB download both backends share.
Closes the parity gap with three structural fixes found via an
ORT-instrumented layer-by-layer diff:

- SwiGLU is packed in ORT's QMoE (gate = even idx, up = odd), not chunk(2)
- No qk_scale in the ONNX attention graph; Q·K goes straight to softmax
- Expert weights are uint4 centered on 8 with implicit zp=8; no XOR

Activations moved to fp16 throughout for ORT semantic match. Drops the
blob format + its Zig parser, Python converter, fixture generators, and
all bf16-based per-kernel fixtures. Correctness now validated by the
cross-backend smoke (span-for-span with tjs) and the public-api spec.

Browser smoke: 2/2 spans agree with transformers.js WebGPU.

add 'secrets' rule preset; union-regex execution

PrivacyFilter.create({ presets: ['secrets'] }) enables a curated set

WebGPU is 2.6-3.7x faster than transformers.js on latency and

bench: drop misleading cold-start comparison

The previous cold-start table compared OPFS vs Cache API storage,
not engines. ORT-Web fetches model bytes inside a worker, so a
main-thread fetch interceptor can't reach them — engine isolation
needs Service-Worker plumbing that isn't worth building. Bench now
reports steady-state per-forward latency only. Docs explain why we
make no cold-start claim and frame the OPFS default as a UX win,
not an engine win.

bench: exclude model download from cold-start measurement

Pre-warm all caches in an untimed prelude, then measure

the redundant second-visit block since the new measurement is already
a true cold start. Updated benchmarks.mdx with the corrected numbers
and explains the 7× tjs gap is storage, not inference (Cache API
rejects payloads above its per-resource limit; OPFS doesn't).

bench: force tjs baseline; refresh measurements in docs

The umbrella's auto-WebGPU short-circuit was making the bench measure

add custom rule engine: regex + match-fn merged with model spans

Rules run alongside the model and emit DetectedSpan with source="rule"
and a "block" | "warn" | "track" severity. They merge with overlap
dedup (rule wins), apply in both batch and streaming paths, and bypass
the enabledCategories filter (custom rules are caller-authored).

add redact(stream) overload mirroring detect(stream)

Same overload pattern: redact(string) keeps the existing batch shape,
redact(AsyncIterable<string>) returns a RedactStreamHandle exposing
three surfaces:

  textStream    AsyncIterable<string>  — yields safe-to-emit redacted
                                         text fragments. Lags the
                                         input by up to safetyMargin
                                         chars; never emits text whose
                                         redaction status is still
                                         pending.
  spanStream    AsyncIterable<DetectedSpan>  — yields stable spans as
                                               they become detectable.
  result        Promise<RedactResult>   — full result on completion.

Mirrors the proxy use case: stream LLM tokens through redact(stream)
and pipe textStream straight to the downstream user. Spans that
straddle the safety edge keep the emit cursor parked at their start
until they're stable, so we never split a redactable region across
two output emits.

stream.ts grows ~250 lines: streamRedact() reuses the trailing-window
inference loop from streamDetect, adds a stable-span list and a
flushTo(targetCursor) helper that applies redactions to the slice
[emitCursor, targetCursor). Multi-consumer queues for both
textStream and spanStream so they can be iterated concurrently.

Test (tests/browser/redact-stream.spec.ts): 24-char chunked stream of
a multi-PII paragraph produces a textStream concatenation that
matches the batch redact() output character-for-character (271
chars, 6 spans). All three streaming-relevant tests pass.

Docs (README, packages/textsift-core/README, docs-site/index.mdx)
updated to show redact(stream) alongside detect(stream).

docs: mark as personal learning project

Adds a clear notice at the top of the root README, both per-package

The notice states the project is built for self-education (WebGPU
shaders, Zig→WASM SIMD, native BPE tokenizer) and that there's no

are welcomed; production support is not on offer.

Sets caller expectations honestly before they install.

collapse startStream() into detect() overload

Single entry point for both batch and streaming. The function picks
behaviour from the type of its first argument:

  // Batch — pass a string, await the result
  const result = await filter.detect("Hi John, my email is x@y.com");
  result.spans;   // DetectedSpan[]

  // Streaming — pass an AsyncIterable<string>, get a handle that
  // exposes both the final result Promise and a span AsyncIterable
  const handle = filter.detect(llmTokenStream);
  for await (const span of handle.spanStream) { ... }
  const final = await handle.result;

Same model as Vercel `streamText`: the handle is sync-returned and
exposes parallel surfaces (Promise + AsyncIterable) backed by one
underlying job. The naming difference is by argument type, not by
function name.

Removed the `startStream()` method and the `DetectStreamSession`
interface — they were the second way to do the same thing. Renamed
`createDetectStream` → `streamDetect`, and `DetectStreamSession` →
`DetectStreamHandle`. Multi-consumer queue inside the streaming
driver lets `spanStream` and `result` both read from the same
underlying span emission stream without losing items.

stream.spec.ts updated to use the new shape; 6/6 spans still match

Docs (README, index.mdx, architecture.mdx, packages/textsift-core/

add startStream() API; rewrite docs in honest tone

Two changes that share a commit because the docs sweep was prompted
by the streaming work being added:

DetectStream API (textsift-core):
  filter.startStream(opts) → DetectStreamSession with append() and
  finish(). Each append() runs the model on a trailing window of the
  accumulated buffer and yields newly-stable spans (those whose end
  is more than safetyMarginTokens behind the trailing edge). finish()
  flushes via the full chunked pipeline. Total work over a stream of
  N tokens is O(N), versus O(N²) for the naive pattern of calling
  detect(buffer) after every chunk arrives. Designed for AI-proxy /
  LLM-output-filtering: stream LLM tokens directly into append(),
  short-circuit on high-confidence span detection.

  New file: packages/textsift-core/src/js/inference/stream.ts.
  New test: tests/browser/stream.spec.ts verifies streaming yields

split into textsift-core + textsift; native BPE tokenizer

Restructures the project as an npm-workspaces monorepo. Two published
packages, one source of truth, same `PrivacyFilter` API on both:

  textsift-core   — lean engine, ~76 KB gzipped. Custom WebGPU + WASM
                    backends, native o200k-style BPE tokenizer, zero
                    runtime dependencies.

  textsift        — umbrella, ~226 KB gzipped. Depends on textsift-core
                    and adds a transformers.js fallback backend so
                    `PrivacyFilter.create()` works on any browser/Node
                    runtime out of the box.

The biggest piece is the native tokenizer in textsift-core that
replaces transformers.js's `AutoTokenizer`. It implements the full
o200k-style BPE pipeline: pretokenizer regex (Unicode-aware, with
`(?i:...)` inline-case-insensitive groups expanded to plain JS
alternatives), GPT-2 byte-level encoding, BPE merge loop with
pretoken caching, vocab + merges loaded from `tokenizer.json`, plus
the 21 added special tokens. Conformance test
(tests/browser/tokenizer-conformance.spec.ts) verifies token-id
sequences match `AutoTokenizer` byte-for-byte across 46 cases
covering English, contractions, code, multi-script Unicode,
emoji + surrogate pairs, special tokens, and edge whitespace.

  packages/textsift-core/    — what was previously `src/`, minus the
    src/js/                    transformers-js.ts backend, minus the
    src/zig/                   AutoTokenizer-based tokenizer.
    src/c/
    scripts/
    package.json             "textsift-core": no runtime deps
  packages/textsift/         — thin wrapper
    src/
      index.ts               re-exports + PrivacyFilter subclass that
                             injects TransformersJsBackend into the
                             "auto" decision via BackendResolver
      transformers-js-backend.ts   ported from the previous backend file
    package.json             "textsift": deps=textsift-core + tjs

PrivacyFilter exposes `createWithResolver(opts, resolver)` so the
umbrella can plug in its transformers.js fallback without textsift-
core having any awareness of transformers.js. textsift-core's
`create()` defaults to webgpu (browsers with f16 adapter) → wasm
fallback. The umbrella's `create()` adds: if neither path works,
use transformers.js.

All 10 playwright tests pass at the new paths. Tokenizer conformance:
46/46 cases match. Bundle sizes verified end-to-end:

  textsift-core minified:        630 KB
  textsift-core minified+gzip:    76 KB

Public class `PrivacyFilter` is unchanged for now — model is still
privacy-filter and existing call sites stay valid. Class will be
