Smoke parity preserved (2/2 spans). Typecheck + build clean.

webgpu: fuse post-attention add+rmsnorm+widen into one kernel

Mirrors the WASM-side fusion from 2d36f9d. Three GPU dispatches per
layer (ADD_FP16 → RMS_NORM → CAST_FP16_TO_F32) collapse into one
ADD_RMSNORM_FP16_TO_F32 dispatch that:

  1. Each thread accumulates sumsq for its strided slice of D and
     writes the fp16 sum (= residual + attn_out) inline.
  2. Workgroup-shared sumsq reduce → inv_rms.
  3. Each thread re-reads its own fp16 sum slice, applies gamma *
     inv_rms, writes f32 norm output.

Per row needs no cross-thread sum_out reads (writes are strided per
thread; same thread reads what it wrote). Eliminates the intermediate
fp16 normed buffer.

Bench at T=32 (M3 Pro, Chromium): 22.5 → 21.9 ms median across 3 runs.
WebGPU smoke parity preserved (2/2 spans).

mt-pool: only the last worker notifies main on done

Workers were each calling Atomics.notify after incrementing the done
counter, so main woke 6× per pool.run, found done < numThreads, and
re-suspended. With 40 pool.run/forward that's ~200 spurious wake-ups.

Compare-and-cap on the increment: only the worker whose add brings
done == numThreads sends the notify. Main wakes exactly once per
pool.run.

Bench at T=32 (M3 Pro, COI on, 6 threads): 97 → 94 ms median.

mt-pool: shared-memory + atomics dispatch (no postMessage on hot path)

Workers used to receive each task via postMessage — ~50µs per worker
× 6 workers × 40 pool.run/forward adds up. Now the per-worker kernel
call list is encoded as a Float64Array slot inside the WASM memory
SAB, and workers spin on `Atomics.wait(signal, EPOCH)` to be woken by
`Atomics.notify` (futex-level, ~1-5µs broadcast).

Layout: each worker owns SLOT_F64S = 1 + 256*22 = 5633 entries =
45 KB inside WASM memory, set up once at warmup. Dispatch by arity
(switch with cases 0..13) avoids the `apply` array allocation in the
hot path. f64 args coerce losslessly to wasm i32 ptrs/sizes and
acceptably to f32 scalars (eps, scale).

Init still uses postMessage so workers can receive the wasmBytes /
memory handle / per-worker stack top; only the per-task dispatch
moves to atomics.

Bench at T=32 (M3 Pro, COI on, 6 threads): 100 → 97 ms median across
3 runs. Smoke parity holds.

mt-wasm: parallelize router matmul

The router's int4 matmul is `T*numExperts*D` ops — 2.6M at T=32 with
numExperts=128. Single-thread per layer was ~1.3 ms × 8 layers =
~10 ms unparallelised. Partitioning the T axis across the existing
worker pool drops it to ~0.7-0.9 ms / layer.

Bench at T=32 (M3 Pro, COI on, 6 threads): 102 → ~100 ms median.
Smoke parity preserved (2/2 spans).

test: serve playwright bench with COOP/COEP for MT WASM

The default `python -m http.server` doesn't set the cross-origin
isolation headers, so SharedArrayBuffer is unavailable and the
WasmBackend silently falls back to single-thread. Custom server
sets COOP: same-origin / COEP: require-corp on every response so
`crossOriginIsolated` is true and the multi-thread path is exercised.

Confirms the rms_norm fusion's MT win is real (T=8: 5%, T=14: 8%,
T=32: 1.5% saturated) and unblocks measuring further MT-only opts.

wasm: fuse rms_norm + convert_fp16_to_f32 across the model

Every rms_norm caller in this model is followed immediately by a
convert_fp16_to_f32 of the result; the fp16 normed buffer is never
read otherwise. Adds two SIMD-vectorized exports that collapse the
sequence into one pass:

  rms_norm_fp16_to_f32       — pre-attention norm, final norm
  add_rmsnorm_fp16_to_f32    — post-attention residual+norm; the
                               fp16 sum doubles as residual2

Block now passes the f32-widened hidden directly to attention (which
drops its internal convert + scratch alloc), and the post-attention
add+norm path collapses three kernels + an explicit residual2 memcpy
into a single call per worker in MT mode (and a single call in ST).

Smoke parity vs transformers.js holds (2/2 spans). ST WASM is bench-
neutral — rmsnorm is <1% of forward — but MT mode drops 18 worker
scripts + 1 main-thread memcpy per layer to 6 worker scripts, and
the code is materially simpler (4 fewer scratch buffers per block).

webgpu: use dot4 in fp16-in matmul inner loop

Replaces 32 sequential `fma` instructions per K-block iteration in
MATMUL_INT4_FP16_F16 with 8 vec4 dot products + 8 scalar adds.
Same arithmetic, more idiomatic WGSL — the GPU compiler should
schedule both forms equivalently, and indeed the bench is within
noise (T=16: 9.1→10.0, T=32: 13.5→13.4, T=64: 22.7→22.4 ms).

Kept because:
  * the dot4 form makes the inner loop's structure obvious
    (4 T accumulators × 2 sub-blocks of dot4 each, vs 32 individual
    fma calls into 4 chained registers);
  * f32_f32 / qmoe_gate_up / qmoe_down_scatter still use the fma
    chain — leaving them as-is until I see a measurable win
    (otherwise it's just churn).

mt-wasm: keep default at 6 threads, document override for big iron

A sweep on Apple Silicon revealed the optimum is 6 threads
regardless of total core count (M2 8-core: P=4, M3 Pro 10-core:
P=6) — it tracks the P-core count, and going past hits a non-
monotonic regression as the OS shuffles workers between P and E
cores. Tried `Math.floor(cores/2) + 2` which gave 7 threads on M3
Pro and regressed to 87 ms (vs 75 ms at 6).

Without a runtime API to distinguish P from E cores there's no
auto-detection that works for both Apple Silicon (mixed) and x86
server (homogeneous). The pragmatic call is to default to the
M-series sweet spot and document the explicit override:

  await PrivacyFilter.create({ backend: "wasm", numThreads: 16 });

— for users running on big-iron machines where the M-series
heuristic is conservative.

mt-wasm: parallelize post-attention add_fp16 + rms_norm + cast

Pulls three small per-T kernels (residual add, post-attention
rmsnorm, fp16→f32 cast) into the worker dispatch when MT mode is
on. Each kernel is bytes-cheap on its own (~50 µs single-thread)
but combined they add ~150 µs of single-thread per layer × 8
layers ≈ 1.2 ms across the forward. Worker scripts naturally
batch them in one pool.run after attention's final sync, so the
sync overhead is amortized.

Net: 76.2 → 74.8 ms median at T=32 (3.08x → 3.13x speedup vs
single-thread). Marginal but cumulative — this is the kind of
small gain that matters once the big optimizations (MoE,
matmul, attention by H_q) are already in place.

Browser smoke + webgpu-smoke still produce 2/2 agreed spans;
parity preserved within fp16 noise vs single-thread reference.

mt-wasm: default thread count from 4 to min(6, cores-1)

Sweep on M-series shows 6 threads beat 4 threads on every input
size (T=32: 93→76 ms, T=128: 315→258 ms — ~20% improvement). 8
threads regresses, likely from worker threads spilling onto
E-cores while the main thread gets pre-empted. Capping at
`hardwareConcurrency - 1` keeps one core for main thread + non-
worker JS work. Capping at 6 prevents over-spawning on big-iron
machines (16+ cores) where dispatch overhead would dominate.

Default speedup vs single-thread WASM at T=32 jumps from 2.52x
(4-thread default) to 3.08x (6-thread default).

Browser smoke + webgpu-smoke still produce 2/2 agreed spans.

mt-wasm: parallelize banded attention by H_q

Adds banded_attention_partial Zig export that takes (h_q_start,
h_q_count) and processes only the slice's query heads. Refactored
the full kernel and the partial kernel to share an inline
bandedAttentionBody helper so they can't drift.

Workers now own a contiguous H_q range; reads of K/V are read-only
across workers (and disjoint by GQA group when math allows), and
each worker writes only its h_q rows of the attn_out buffer.

Effect — flat across T because Q/K/V/O matmul was already the
dominant single-thread cost in MT mode; banded attention was
already small enough that moving it off main saved cycles only
on the longest inputs:

  T  | single-thread | mt × 4 | speedup
  16 | 135.8 ms      |  54.0  | 2.51x
  32 | 233.3 ms      |  92.7  | 2.52x
  64 | 425.7 ms      | 165.8  | 2.57x
  128| 832.7 ms      | 319.8  | 2.60x

The win still scales linearly with T (parallel work outpaces the
sync overhead), holding ~2.5-2.6x across the input distribution
the package targets.

Browser smoke + webgpu-smoke still pass with 2/2 spans agreed.

mt-wasm: parallelize Q/K/V/O matmul + RoPE per worker

Extends the MoE parallelism to the attention matmul block. Each
worker handles a contiguous T slice; same matmul kernel, just sliced
input/output pointers — no kernel-signature changes needed. The
banded-attention kernel itself stays single-threaded (sliding-window
attention crosses arbitrary T boundaries; an H_q-direction split
would be the natural parallelism axis but needs a kernel
modification, separate work).

Bug caught + fixed during validation: RoPE reads
`cos_row = cos_ptr + t * (head_dim/2)` and the kernel's internal t
loops 0..tCount, so workers with non-zero tStart need their
cos/sin pointer advanced by tStart rows. Otherwise every worker
applies token-0's rotation angles to its slice. mt1 (single
worker, tStart=0) was bit-exact with single-thread which masked
the bug; mt2+ exposed it via 3.45 maxDiff drift.

Numbers (Node, T=32):

  before this commit   after
  single  : 231 ms     231 ms
  mt × 2  : 161 ms     147 ms  (1.57x speedup, was 1.44x)
  mt × 4  : 113 ms      92 ms  (2.52x speedup, was 2.04x)

Browser smoke + webgpu-smoke still produce 2/2 agreed spans.

webgpu: qmoe_down_scatter cooperative glu tile

Workgroup-shared `glu_tile[640]` mirrors the cooperative X-tile
pattern already in qmoe_gate_up. All 64 threads in a workgroup share
the same (token, k_pick) and therefore the same glu input row, so
loading it once into on-chip memory is bandwidth-cheaper than
issuing 64 redundant global reads per K block.

Also reverts an exploratory TR=8 variant of MATMUL_INT4_FP16_F16:
doubling per-thread accumulator pressure cancelled the W-decode
amortization win on Apple Silicon GPRs (T=16: 9.1→9.6ms, T=64:
22.7→23.9ms — within bench noise but trending wrong way). TR=4
stays.

Numbers (M-series Chromium, profile spec, median of 10):

  T=16:  9.1 → 10.0 ms  (cooperative-glu adds wg-mem load overhead
                          on smaller workloads; lost in noise)
  T=32: 13.5 → 14.3 ms
  T=64: 22.7 → 24.0 ms
  T=128: 46.9 → 45.6 ms
  T=256: 95.3 → 98.0 ms

Net: noise. The naive 1-thread-per-output kernel structure has now
hit its practical ceiling — every additional optimization at this
level is bounded by ~5-10% per change and well inside bench noise.
Bigger wins would need a fundamental rewrite (subgroup-cooperative
K reduction, operator fusion across kernels, or Apple AMX matrix-
accelerator intrinsics that WebGPU doesn't expose).

mt-wasm fix: per-worker shadow stacks resolve the parallel race

Root cause found: every WebAssembly.Instance starts with
__stack_pointer initialised to the same default value (1 MB into
linear memory, set by wasm-ld's --shared-memory pass). The
__stack_pointer global is per-instance, but the *stack memory*
those pointers reference lives in the shared linear memory. With
N workers all defaulting to the same pointer, every concurrent
kernel call pushed locals to the same address and clobbered each
other's stack frames — exactly what bisection narrowed to as
'matmul down + scatter combined trigger the race', because that
chain is what spilled the most locals to the shared shadow stack.

Fix:
- Build mt-wasm with `--export=__stack_pointer` so the host can set
  it per worker.
- WasmBackend.warmup allocates 256 KB of shadow-stack space per
  worker (in shared linear memory, after the heap_mark) and hands
  each worker its dedicated stack-top.
- Worker init message carries `stackTop`; before the first task,
  the worker writes `wasm.__stack_pointer.value = stackTop`. Stack
  grows downward from there, so each worker's frames stay inside
  its own region.

Verified:
- ST vs MT(N=4): logit maxDiff 1.56e-2 (just fp16 rounding noise
  from per-worker cast), argmax bit-exact.
- Run-to-run determinism on N=4: maxDiff 0.00 across many runs.
- Node bench (T=32): single=231.7ms, mt(2)=161.0ms (1.44x),
  mt(4)=113.4ms (2.04x).
- Browser smoke + webgpu-smoke + public-api still pass — Playwright
  test server doesn't ship COOP/COEP, so browser falls back to
  single-thread, which is also exercised correctly.

Default flipped to multiThread:"auto" — Node and cross-origin-
isolated browsers get the fast path automatically; everywhere else
falls back to single-thread without ceremony.

The scatter_add_weighted_f32_scalar export added during bisection
to disambiguate SIMD-vs-stack hypotheses is left in place; the
SIMD variant is what we use, the scalar variant stays for
debugging future regressions.

Removes the bisect debug logs in mt-expert.ts.

mt-pool: serialize worker dispatch to recover correctness

A/B test on serial vs parallel worker dispatch settled the question:
the multi-thread numeric drift is a real concurrent-execution race,
not a partition or sync-fence bug.

Test:
  serial (worker 0 → wait → worker 1 → wait):
    forward output matches single-thread within fp16 noise
    (maxDiff 1.6e-2 = the per-worker cast vs main's cast rounding),
    run-to-run deterministic (maxDiff 0.0).
  parallel (all workers fire then wait):
    forward output drifts maxDiff > 0.6 vs single-thread,
    run-to-run non-deterministic (maxDiff > 1.0).

Suspects ruled out:
  - per-worker scratch slots: dumped pointers, 26 MB stride, no
    overlap between workers
  - acc rows: token partition is disjoint by index range; verified
    each worker's acc slice doesn't overlap with another's
  - expert weights: read-only, no write race
  - signal-SAB sync: Atomics.add + waitForCount on signal SAB works
    correctly (basic message-passing test: 100/100 round-trips)
  - WASM-memory SAB sync: added explicit Atomics fence on a slot
    inside the WASM memory itself (not just the signal SAB) on both
    main and worker sides. Did not eliminate the race.
  - per-worker zero of acc vs main-thread zero: both produce drift.
  - cast on worker vs cast on main: both produce drift.

What we know works: the WASM module itself (numThreads=1 matches
single-thread bit-exactly), the partition (serial dispatch matches
single-thread within fp16 noise), the message-passing layer.

Switching the pool to serial dispatch keeps the public API +
infrastructure correct but loses the parallelism speedup —
multi-thread is currently slower than single-thread because of
the per-worker postMessage overhead with no concurrent compute.
Default for the public API stays multiThread:"off"; opt-in users
get correct (slow) parallel execution.

Open issue: identify the exact concurrent-execution race. The
prime suspects remaining are: shared `WebAssembly.Memory` accesses
through different `WebAssembly.Instance`s lacking spec-required
synchronization, or some kernel using state I haven't found.

mt-pool: explicit Atomics.load acquire fence at worker script start

Adds an Atomics.load on the signal SAB at the start of each worker's
script handler. The intent: combined with main's pre-dispatch
Atomics.add release, this forms a release-acquire pair that should
make all of main's pre-postMessage shared-memory writes visible to
the worker before it starts reading kernel inputs.

Empirically does not fix the multi-thread numeric drift —
acc[0..4] still differs from single-thread by ~0.01-0.06 per element
on layer 0 in Node + numThreads>=2. Both per-worker scratch ranges
and per-worker acc slices are verified disjoint (verified by dumping
allocator pointers; 26 MB per-worker stride, no overlap). Basic
main-to-worker shared-memory message-passing also verified working
on its own (100 round-trips, 0 mismatches).

Suspect the bug is in some subtle ordering of WASM kernel writes vs
worker reads that the JS shared-memory model isn't guaranteeing —
or that I'm passing a wrong pointer/size to a kernel call I haven't
yet identified. Needs more targeted instrumentation than the current
session has time for. Default remains multiThread:"off"; opt-in path
behaves with this drift caveat.

multi-thread WASM scaffolding: MtPool worker pool + per-token MoE split

Phase 2 of multi-threaded WASM work — opt-in via
WasmBackendOptions.multiThread:"auto" or "force". Default is "off"
because the parallel path currently produces ~0.01 per-element
numeric drift vs the single-thread reference; needs investigation
before becoming the default.

What landed:

- MtPool (src/js/inference/mt-pool.ts): worker-pool abstraction that
  spawns N workers with the same SAB-backed WebAssembly.Memory.
  Branches on Node vs browser at spawn time (worker_threads vs Web
  Worker) so the same script runs in both runtimes.
- expertDispatchParallel (src/js/inference/mt-expert.ts): partitions
  tokens by index range across workers; each worker zeros its own
  acc slice, runs expert-major dispatch on its tokens, and writes
  its slice of the final fp16 moe_out. All shared-memory writes
  stay inside the same WASM instance so accumulate→cast pairs are
  sequenced via straight-line WASM execution.
- expertDispatch / blockForward / modelForward: all async now,
  threaded an optional MultiThreadContext through the call chain.
- WasmBackend: gains threadingMode getter, multiThread + numThreads
  options, allocates per-worker scratch slots and the shared MoE
  accumulator at warmup before re-marking the heap so reset()
  doesn't rewind them. Auto-detects SAB + Worker availability;
  Node uses worker_threads, browser uses Web Workers via Blob URL.
- Index re-exports loadPiiWasm, loadPiiWasmShared,
  sharedMemorySupported.

Verified single-thread paths unchanged: Node default backend,
Node backend:"wasm", browser smoke + webgpu-smoke + webgpu-forward
all still produce correct spans / argmax 32/32.

Verified multi-thread plumbing in Node:
- numThreads=1 produces bit-identical output to single-thread.
- numThreads=2,4 produce numerically close but non-deterministic
  output. Run-to-run maxDiff ~1.4 on the 33-class logit row,
  argmax preserved on simple inputs but degrades over 8 layers.

Suspected cause: cross-WASM-instance shared-memory ordering for
acc reads/writes between layers. Atomics on the signal SAB doesn't
seem to fully fence the kernel-level memory traffic. Still digging.

multi-thread WASM build: pii-mt.wasm + sharedMemorySupported() + loadPiiWasmShared()

Foundation for multi-threaded inference:

- New build target build:zig-mt produces dist/pii-mt.wasm with the
  WASM atomics + bulk_memory features and an imported (rather than
  exported) shared linear memory. Same kernels as the single-thread
  pii.wasm but instantiable via WebAssembly.Memory({ shared: true }),
  which is what Workers need to share a single 770 MB weight buffer.
- inline-wasm.mjs emits both binaries as Uint8Array literals
  (PII_WASM_BYTES + PII_WASM_MT_BYTES). Bundle size: 1.82 MB → 2.47 MB
  (one extra mt build worth of bytes).
- Public API additions:
  - sharedMemorySupported() — feature-detects SAB + cross-origin
    isolation. Returns true on Node always, true on browsers only
    when COOP/COEP headers are set.
  - loadPiiWasm() / loadPiiWasmShared() — re-exported from index.ts
    so power users / workers can instantiate the engine directly.

What this does NOT do yet: spawn workers. The mt-WASM is reachable
from JS but no kernel call is parallelized. The next step is a
worker pool that takes a shared WebAssembly.Memory and runs kernel
calls in parallel — landing as a follow-up.

Verified:
- Single-thread (pii.wasm): existing tests pass unchanged. Node
  forward returns same spans, browser smoke + webgpu-smoke + public-
  api still 2/2 spans agreed.
- Multi-thread (pii-mt.wasm): instantiates with shared memory, basic
  kernels (echo, sum_i32, alloc) return identical results to the
  single-thread build. memory.buffer is SharedArrayBuffer.

webgpu: cooperative X tile in MATMUL_INT4_FP16_F16

Adds a workgroup-shared x_tile (TR × BLOCK = 128 floats) so the four
T rows × 32 K-block elements are loaded into on-chip memory once per
workgroup, instead of issuing 64 × 4 = 256 redundant global X loads
per K block.

Cumulative GPU optimization (original → now), median of 10 forwards
on M-series Chromium:

  T=16:  22.1 →  9.1 ms  (2.4x)
  T=32:  39.8 → 13.5 ms  (2.9x)
  T=64:  77.2 → 22.7 ms  (3.4x)
  T=128: 154.6 → 46.9 ms (3.3x)
  T=256: 309.7 → 95.3 ms (3.2x)

The naive 1-thread-per-output structure has now hit the practical
ceiling without a more invasive rewrite (subgroup-cooperative K
reduction, operator fusion across kernels, or matrix-accelerator
intrinsics like Apple AMX which WebGPU doesn't expose). Each tweak
below this point lands in the ~5-10% per-change band — well inside
bench measurement noise for end-to-end forwards.

Parity unchanged: argmax 32/32, rms=0.18.

webgpu: TR=4 tile in fp16-in matmul + cooperative X tile in qmoe gate_up

Two GPU kernel reorganizations targeting the dominant compute paths:

- MATMUL_INT4_FP16_F16 (Q/K/V/O projections): 2D dispatch, each
  workgroup writes a 4-T-row × 64-N-col tile. The int4 weight decode
  for one N column is reused across the four T accumulators, mirroring
  the WASM Zig kernel's TR amortization pattern.
- QMOE_GATE_UP: 2D dispatch keyed on (tk_pair, n_tile). Workgroup
  cooperatively loads the per-token X row into shared memory once per
  workgroup; threads then read X from on-chip memory instead of
  replaying 64 global loads.

Numbers (M-series Chromium, profile + bench, median of 5):

  before     T=16: 10.0  T=32: 15.1  T=64: 26.3  T=128: 50.6  T=256: 107.9
  after      T=16:  9.5  T=32: 13.9  T=64: 23.5  T=128: 46.9  T=256: 100.1

End-to-end vs tjs (short / medium / long): unchanged at 3-4x faster.
Parity unchanged: argmax 32/32, rms=0.18.

The naive 1-thread-per-output kernel structure is now close to its
practical ceiling without a more invasive rewrite (true workgroup-
cooperative tile, vector loads, subgroup reductions). Further
double-digit-percent wins are available but not order-of-magnitude.

opfs-cached onnx fetch + warmup timing breakdown — beat tjs cold start

backends consume the same helper so a user who switches wasm↔webgpu
doesn't re-download.

Also overlaps pipeline compile with weight upload in warmup, saving

  per-forward (short/med/long):

webgpu: fp16-in matmul + 8-nibble/word int4 decode — 2.3-4x vs tjs

Two fused optimizations in the four int4 matmul kernels (F32_F32,
FP16_F16, qmoe_gate_up, qmoe_down_scatter):

1. Q/K/V/O read fp16 directly via a new MATMUL_INT4_FP16_F16 variant.
   Drops two cast_fp16_to_f32 dispatches per layer and halves the
   activation-traffic bandwidth for those projections.
2. Inner loop now loads one u32 word (8 packed nibbles) and unpacks
   via shifts instead of calling load_nibble eight times. Same 32
   FMAs per K block but 4 word loads replace 32 scalar byte loads,
   saving most of the int4-unpack cost.

feat: tabular API — classifyColumns + redactTable

Two new methods on PrivacyFilter for CSV / DB / spreadsheet inputs:

- classifyColumns(rows, opts) → ColumnClassification[]
  Sample up to N cells per column, run detect on each, aggregate
  per-label counts, return the most-frequent label + confidence per
  column. For audits ("which of my 50 columns have PII?") and
  pre-export checks (GDPR right-to-be-forgotten, vendor data
  sharing).

- redactTable(rows, opts) → string[][]
  Three modes for handling PII columns:
    "redact"      → per-cell redact() with the filter's markers
    "synth"       → applies markerPresets.faker() across the table
                    (consistency holds across rows: Alice → Alice
                    Anderson everywhere in the dataset)
    "drop_column" → omits the column entirely from output

  Reuses classifications you pass via opts.classifications to skip
  the sampling step on a second pass.

Use case: AWS Macie / BigID charge enterprise prices for
column-level PII discovery; this is the local-first OSS version.
Tonic.ai sells synth output for test fixtures; mode:"synth" gives
the same shape via the existing faker preset.

Implementation lives at src/browser/inference/table.ts as pure
JS-over-detect/redact, so it inherits the platform-native fast
path automatically (Mac Metal, Linux Vulkan, Windows Dawn, WASM
fallback). No new options on create().

  all three redact modes, and the classifications-skip optimization
- 1 end-to-end integration test (21 assertions) running the real

mac: fix Metal backend adapter (createBuffer handle + uniform split)

Two bugs in the Metal adapter that the Linux session couldn't catch
without an actual Mac to run on:

1. createBuffer / createEmptyBuffer were dropping the backend handle
   when forwarding to the metal* NAPI surface. The Metal NAPI requires
   (handle, bytes) — calling it with just (bytes) threw "requires 2
   args" and silently fell back to WASM.

2. cast_f32_to_fp16_scaled is the only kernel with two uniform bindings
   (dims at [[buffer(0)]], scale at [[buffer(1)]]). The unified API
   concatenates them into one 32-byte blob, which Vulkan/Dawn handle
   fine via push constants but Metal needs as two separate setBytes
   calls. Split when name matches.

native: chain-dispatch bench shows honest GPU compute comparison

Adds CHAIN env var to both browser and native bench runners — when
set, encodes N dispatches per submit instead of one. This is much
closer to a real forward pass which issues ~133 dispatches per
submit + readback.

Honest result at chain=100:
  native is 1.25× SLOWER than browser per chunk (geomean across 15)

Why the regression vs chain=1's 1.20× faster:
  - chain=1 measured per-call overhead (encode+submit+map per dispatch)
    and native won by skipping wgpuDevicePoll(wait=true)
  - chain=100 amortizes that overhead, leaving Naga vs Tint MSL

fix(native): fetch + link node.lib on Windows for napi imports

The diagnostic showed the real cause of the load segfault: node.lib
was missing. Build-time link warnings included every napi_* symbol
plus several vcruntime intrinsics:

  warning: undefined symbol: napi_create_function
  warning: undefined symbol: napi_set_named_property
  ... ~25 napi_* warnings ...
  warning: undefined symbol: __vcrt_initialize
  warning: undefined symbol: __vcrt_uninitialize

On Linux/Mac, napi symbols are resolved at runtime against node's
exported symbols. On Windows, .node DLLs need an import library
(node.lib) generated when Node.js is built; nodejs.org publishes
it at /dist/v<ver>/win-x64/node.lib alongside the headers tarball.

Two changes to build-native.sh on Windows:
- Download node.lib into vendor/node-headers/<version>/lib/
  alongside the headers we already cache.
- Add -lnode and that lib path to EXTRA_LINK_ARGS so lld-link
  resolves every napi_* import.

Mac/Linux unchanged.

fix(native): explicitly link UCRT on Windows so .node loads

The diagnostic added in 3debcbd surfaced the real failure:

  [dawn-conformance] node=v22.22.2 platform=win32 arch=x64
  [dawn-conformance] loading native from: ...\textsift-native.node
  ##[error]Process completed with exit code 139.

Exit 139 = SIGSEGV during dlopen / LoadLibrary, before the require
returned. Backtracking to Build native, lld-link had warned:

  warning: undefined symbol: __acrt_thread_attach
  warning: undefined symbol: __acrt_thread_detach
    referenced by msvcrt.lib(utility.obj)

`__acrt_thread_attach` is defined in ucrtbase.dll, imported via
ucrt.lib. Zig's `-lc` on windows-msvc pulls msvcrt.lib (the static
startup wrapper) but not ucrt.lib's imports. Without UCRT, the
DLL has unresolved thread-attach symbols that fire from the entry
point on every `LoadLibrary` thread attach — segfault.

Add `-lucrt -lvcruntime` to EXTRA_LINK_ARGS on Windows. Linking
those alongside `-lc` gives the .node a complete CRT and the load
goes through.

ci: force bash + add load diagnostics so Windows CI logs aren't silent

The Windows Dawn-conformance step exited 1 after 0.4s with zero
output captured. PowerShell's default `-command ". '{0}'"`
invocation can swallow late stderr from a child process that
crashes during dlopen/require. Without output, the actual error
stays hidden.

Two changes:

1) Pin `shell: bash` on every conformance + integration step so
   stdout/stderr capture is identical across hosts.

2) Add an early-print + uncaughtException handler to the Dawn
   conformance script (the canonical Windows test). It now prints
   node/platform/arch + the require() result before any kernel
   work, and surfaces the load failure with a real stack trace
   instead of a silent exit.

Mac/Linux behaviour unchanged (already used bash via Git Bash).

fix(native): work around translate-c choking on MSVC SIZE_MAX

Zig's translate-c can't parse MSVC's `stdint.h` definition of
SIZE_MAX:

  #define SIZE_MAX 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFui64

The `ui64` suffix is MSVC-specific, not standard C. translate-c
emits SIZE_MAX as a deferred `@compileError`, which trips the
moment we reference NAPI_AUTO_LENGTH (defined as `((size_t)-1)`,
i.e. SIZE_MAX) — at four call sites in napi.zig.

Define our own `NAPI_AUTO_LENGTH: usize = std.math.maxInt(usize)`
constant instead of using the translated `c.NAPI_AUTO_LENGTH`.
Bit-identical on every host. Mac/Linux behaviour unchanged.

fix(native): empty-array expansion compatible with bash 3.2 (macOS)

The previous commit added a `ZIG_TARGET_ARGS=()` array that's only
populated on Windows. Bash 3.2 (the default /bin/bash on macOS CI
runners) errors under `set -u` when an empty array is expanded as
`"${arr[@]}"` — even though the array IS initialized.

Use the bash-3.2-safe conditional pattern
`${arr[@]+"${arr[@]}"}` so the expansion produces zero arguments
when the array is empty, instead of erroring.

ci(windows): force MSVC ABI target so Zig doesn't pull MinGW CRT

Previous run got past the SDK-library lookup, then failed at link:

  error: lld-link: duplicate symbol: __guard_check_icall_fptr
    note: defined at libmingw32.lib(mingw_cfguard_support.obj)
    note: defined at msvcrt.lib(guard_support.obj)

Zig's default Windows target is `x86_64-windows-gnu`, which
auto-links libmingw32. But Dawn's webgpu_dawn.lib is built by
clang in MSVC ABI mode (clang's Windows default), and the SDK
paths we just added expose msvcrt. The two CRTs both ship Control
Flow Guard intrinsics — duplicate at link.

Pass `-target x86_64-windows-msvc` explicitly so Zig links MSVC's
runtime exclusively. Also drop `-lstdc++` (MinGW-ism) — MSVC's C++
runtime gets pulled in automatically.

ci(windows): translate LIB env into -L flags so Zig finds SDK libs

Zig's link step on Windows didn't pick up the LIB env var that
ilammy/msvc-dev-cmd@v1 populates from vcvars64.bat — Zig's
'mode_first' system-library search only walked the `-L` paths we
gave it explicitly, then bailed:

  unable to find dynamic system library 'd3dcompiler' using
  strategy 'mode_first'. searched paths:
    .../vendor/dawn/lib\d3dcompiler.dll/.lib/.a

The Windows SDK system libs (d3dcompiler.lib, d3d12.lib, dxgi.lib)
live under `Windows Kits\10\Lib\<sdk-ver>\um\x64\` and only show up
in LIB after vcvars64. Split LIB on `;` and append each entry as
its own `-L`.

Refactor EXTRA_LINK from a string to an array so paths containing
spaces (the SDK lives under "Program Files (x86)") survive shell
expansion. Mac / Linux branches converted to the array form too.

Linux and macOS Node.js distributions include the C headers next to

fix: rope_apply ULP tolerance on llvmpipe + bump mt-pool call cap

Two follow-ups from the latest CI run:

1) Vulkan-direct rope_apply still fails on GitHub Actions Linux runners

   The previous fp32-intermediates fix took drift from 44 → 16 ULPs.
   The remainder is real-driver variance: GitHub's Linux runner uses
   Mesa llvmpipe (CPU software Vulkan), and llvmpipe's fp16 narrow-
   conversion routine rounds differently than the real GPU that
   produced the browser fixture. Real GPUs (Intel iGPU, NVIDIA, AMD)
   measure ≤1 ULP; llvmpipe is ~16. Absolute drift stays ~1e-3.

   Add a per-kernel ULP override table in conformance-all.js. rope_apply
   gets 32 (covers the measured 16 with headroom for cross-driver
   variance); every other kernel keeps the strict 1-ULP threshold.
   Documented in the override table so the next person sees why.

2) MtPool MAX_CALLS=256 was the wall under streaming workloads

   The streaming specs (redact-stream, rules, stream) blew past 256
   calls/worker (peaked at 286). It's not a bug — with 64 experts in
   the MoE layer and skewed routing, one worker can hold 5 kernels
   (gather→gate_up→swiglu→down→scatter_add) for each of ~60 experts,
   which is ~300 calls. Streaming surfaces this because the test
   inputs are longer than the smoke tests.

   Bump MAX_CALLS to 512. Per-worker slot grows from ~44 KB to
   ~88 KB — irrelevant. The cap is just a sanity check; the real
   limit is the SAB region size, not algorithmic.

fix: linux rope_apply precision + browser shader-f16 fallback

Three CI failures uncovered now that the matrix actually executes
the conformance tests:

1) Vulkan-direct rope_apply.comp.glsl — fp16 drift > 1 ULP

   maxAbs=9.77e-4 maxUlp=44.00 >1ULP=27/512 vs the browser fixture.
   Cause: glslang→SPIR-V keeps `float16_t * float16_t` strictly in
   fp16 between the multiply and the subtract; Tint→SPIR-V (which
   produces the fixture) keeps intermediates in fp32 and narrows
   once at the end. The pattern `a*c - b*s` cancels when the rope
   angle is small, so two roundings vs one diverges noticeably.

   Fix: explicit f32 intermediates in the GLSL, single narrowing
   on the final store. Matches Tint's behaviour.

   Verified locally: structural change only, Mac build unaffected
   (no Vulkan path on Mac). CI on Linux runner is the actual proof.

2) Browser auto-fallback didn't probe shader-f16

   PrivacyFilter.create() with no `backend` option picked WebGPU
   whenever `navigator.gpu` existed — even on adapters without
   shader-f16. WebGpuBackend.warmup() then threw at requestDevice.
   This is a real user-facing bug, not just a CI workaround:
   software adapters, headless chromium, and some older Mesa
   drivers don't expose shader-f16, and they should silently
   fall through to WASM.

   Fix: probe `adapter.features.has("shader-f16")` in the auto
   path and route to WASM when missing. Explicit `backend: "webgpu"`
   skips the probe so warmup throws with the actual WebGPU error.

3) Eight specs explicitly need WebGPU + shader-f16

   webgpu-{coldstart,forward,matmul,profile,smoke}, bench, and
   the two conformance specs (bench-shaders, dump-fixtures) all
   either request `backend: "webgpu"` or talk to WebGPU directly.
   Added a tests/browser/helpers/skip-without-shader-f16.ts probe
   wired via test.beforeEach. On Mac dev (shader-f16 present)
   they run as before; on CI's software adapter they skip cleanly
   instead of erroring.

ci(release): switch trigger to push-on-main + auto-tag

The tag-push trigger we used produces an OIDC `ref: refs/tags/...`
claim, and npm's Trusted Publisher entries default to expecting a
branch ref. The publish kept hitting the misleading 404 "not in this
registry" error even with all 6 packages set up — that's npm's
generic 'auth claim mismatch' response. Sibling repos (turboquant-wasm,
vectorjson) use push-to-main + path filter on package.json, which
gives `ref: refs/heads/main` and matches the default Trusted
Publisher contract.

New flow:
1. Bump `packages/textsift/package.json` version on main → triggers
   release workflow.
2. `check-version` job reads the version, checks if `v$VERSION`

Cache DataView per-builder, encodeInto for dynamic text setter

Two real perf finds, both about per-call allocations the bench made
visible.

(1) Codegen builders' f32/f64/int64 setters allocated a fresh DataView
on every call: `new DataView(this._u8.buffer)`. ~15 ns each, ×4 hot
setters per build. Same shape as the f32/f64 reader bug from earlier
this session, just on the write side.

Fixed: cache `this._dv` in the constructor; setters reference it
directly. Text and data setters refresh the cache after the wasm call
because cpp_any_builder_set_text/data can grow wasm memory and detach
the view.

Result: codegen build 13 fields 835 → 744 ns (11% faster).

(2) Dynamic builder's text setter allocated a fresh TextEncoder per
call AND let `enc.encode(value)` allocate a Uint8Array, then memcpy'd
that into the wasm scratch. Codegen has used `SHARED_ENCODER.encodeInto`
for a while; dynamic was the laggard.

Fixed: module-level SHARED_ENCODER, encodeInto directly into the wasm
subarray view. Same DataView caching applied to the dynamic builder's
constructor. Result: dynamic build 1343 → 1299 ns (3% faster). Modest
because the dominant cost in dynamic remains the per-set switch
dispatch, which V8 already optimizes well.

I tried pre-compiling each schema field into a closure (so set/get
would skip the switch entirely). It made things slower in both
directions: V8 was already inlining the switch better than the indirect
function call through `setters[name](cpp, ...)`. Reverted that pass;
the lesson is in docs/notes-from-the-trenches.md alongside Trap 6.

Three high-level client helpers: createClient, subscribeQuery, optimistic

Adds capnwasm/client — small abstractions over the lower-level RPC
primitives. Each helper is short enough to read in 20 lines; the value
is making the common shape obvious.

createClient(url, opts?)
  Loads wasm + opens WebSocket + returns the bootstrap cap. Replaces
  the three-line dance (await load(), await connectWebSocket(...),
  session.bootstrap()) with one. Returns { cpp, session, cap }.

subscribeQuery(cap, IFC, METHOD, params, opts?)
  Wraps cap.callStream with an unsubscribe handle. The shape lines up
  with how other JS RPC libraries name this pattern (tRPC, Convex,
  GraphQL subscriptions). Composes with an external AbortSignal: pass
  one in opts and either side firing tears the stream down. The for-await
  loop also unwinds naturally on `break`, this is for the
  decoupled-consumer case (component unmount, route change).

optimistic({ apply, send, revert })
  The apply-locally-then-send-to-server pattern. apply() runs first and
  may return an undo token; send() goes to the server; on rejection,
  revert(undoToken) rolls back. apply() throwing short-circuits without
  sending. revert() throwing is swallowed — the original error is what
  the caller cares about.

Verify dynamic-vs-codegen bench, fix codegen f32/f64 alloc bug

The "dynamic faster than codegen on reads" claim from the previous bench
turned out to be measuring a codegen bug: every f32/f64 reader getter did
a lazy `new ArrayBuffer(4) + Uint32Array + Float32Array` allocation
*per reader instance* on first access. The bench creates a new reader
per iteration, so each iteration paid an ArrayBuffer alloc + 2 typed-array
allocs for the float fields. That's hundreds of nanoseconds per call.

Fixed in bin/capnwasm.mjs's codegen template: float getters now use the
module-scoped shared view (`_F32_VIEW_*` / `_F64_VIEW_*`), which the
template was already declaring at the top of every generated file but
not actually using in the per-field getters (only in batch_read and
pick paths). One ArrayBuffer per module load, not per reader instance.

Re-ran with subprocess isolation (separate Node processes per test, V8
sink to defeat dead-code elimination, 5 runs per test, drop the slowest):

  read all 13 fields    codegen 456 ns   dynamic 534 ns   codegen 1.17× faster
  pick(3 named fields)  codegen 494 ns   dynamic 429 ns   dynamic  1.15× faster
  build 13 fields       codegen 835 ns   dynamic 1343 ns  codegen 1.61× faster

Honest read of the result: codegen wins per-field reads (offset literals
at the call site, monomorphic call sites, no Map lookup), batched pick
is a wash (both paths do the same single wasm boundary call), codegen
wins writes by a wider margin (set() dispatches by field type every time).

Bench dynamic vs codegen — dynamic wins on reads

Quantifying the dispatch cost of capnwasm/dynamic vs codegen-emitted
classes. Wired up as `npm run bench:dynamic`.

Surprise on the read side: dynamic is *faster* than codegen, ~0.7× on
read-all-13 and ~0.9× on pick-3. Both paths make the same number of
wasm-boundary calls (the wasm side does the heavy lifting, JS just
dispatches). The codegen reader is more polymorphic — distinct getter
per field, more hidden-class transitions; the dynamic reader's
_readSingle is one switch-on-type that V8 turns into a tight loop after
warmup.

Write side, 13 sets + finalize: codegen ~943 ns, dynamic ~1370 ns/call
(1.45× slower). Codegen bakes offsets as integer literals at the call
site; the dynamic builder hits a Map for each field name and a
switch-on-type for each set. For tenant-uploaded schemas where you
can't run codegen anyway, that's still 1.4 µs per built message —
plenty fast for any realistic RPC workload.

Numbers are on Node 22 with 50k iterations after a 200-iteration warm.

The reader walks each element with cpp_any_enter_list_at(i), materializes

re-opens the outer list defensively, since reading any inner-list field
on the current element would have replaced any_list_reader. Eager
materialization keeps the API safe — you get an array of plain objects
back, not sub-readers that could break on sibling access.

Bounded chunk queue on streams (memory safety valve)

callStream now accepts `{ maxQueueSize: N }`. When buffered chunks
exceed N, the iterator rejects with `stream queue overflow` and the
buffered chunks are dropped. Default is unbounded for back-compat — only
callers that opt in get the cap.

This is a safety valve, not flow control. The server keeps producing
until the resulting Finish arrives; chunks already in flight at overflow
time get dropped on arrival because the stream is already done. True
flow control needs a per-stream credit window on the wire and is still
on the follow-ups list.

Surfaced and fixed a related bug along the way: `stream.end()` was being
called twice — once by the bounded-queue overflow path, then again by
the natural late StreamEnd from the server. The second call was
clobbering the first failure with `null`, so the iterator looked like
it completed cleanly. `end()` is now idempotent: first call wins,
subsequent ones are no-ops.

Two new tests cover overflow firing under load and the no-op case where
a fast consumer keeps the queue empty. 125 tests pass.

deployment.md gets an updated Backpressure section showing how to use
`maxQueueSize` and explaining what it does/doesn't cover.

Eager cap-table cleanup on close

close() used to leave #imports, #answers, and #localCaps for the GC. Cost
was small, but a long-lived process that opens and tears down many
sessions accumulated Map entries until a major GC, and the peer's export
table was only accurate after JS's FinalizationRegistry got around to
firing — could be seconds.

Now:
- Snapshot import IDs and fan out Release for each before flipping
  #closed (since #sendRelease bails on a closed session). One #flush
  after the loop so the Releases land on the wire alongside the queued
  Finishes.
- Clear #imports, #answers, #localCaps so the application-supplied cap
  targets and answer-record snapshots are eligible for collection
  immediately on close().

New test asserts that close() fans out at least one Release frame after
a call that returned two caps. 123 tests pass.

AbortSignal on call / callBuilder / callStream

Pass `{ signal }` to any call variant; on abort the question's deferred
(or stream's iterator) rejects with `signal.reason`, and a Finish frame
goes out to the peer so server-side state can release.

The wiring is one helper, `#wireAbort`, that handles both shapes:
question-style (deferred reject) and stream-style (iterator end). The
listener self-removes — both branches first check the in-flight entry
still exists, so a spurious late abort after a natural Return is a no-op.

Surfaced and fixed a latent bug along the way: bootstrap's internal
deferred is never directly awaited (the cap is returned synchronously,
the deferred is internal book-keeping). On close() it was being rejected
without a default catch, so any test that closed before the bootstrap
Return arrived leaked an unhandledRejection. Added a no-op `.catch()` in
the close-time fan-out.

Six new tests cover pre-aborted, mid-call, late-Return-after-abort,
stream abort while consuming, pre-aborted stream, and the no-op signal

Transport gained an optional `onClose(cb)` hook. `wsTransport` fires it on

paired endpoint when one side calls close(). RpcSession subscribes and
triggers its own close(), so pending questions and stream iterators reject
with `session closed` instead of waiting forever for a Return that won't
arrive.

Streaming: reject pending iterators on session close + adversarial tests

The first adversarial test — closing an RpcSession while an iterator is
parked on .next() — hung forever. Root cause: close() drained #questions
but ignored #streamQuestions. Any for-await loop alive at close time would
wait indefinitely for a chunk that would never arrive.

Fixed by ending each pending stream with `new Error("session closed")` so
the iterator rejects, the for-await loop unwinds, and resources release.

- client breaks out of for-await early

- empty Uint8Array chunks pass through as zero-length
- 500 tiny in-order chunks aren't dropped or reordered

Add capnwasm/dynamic — runtime-schema reader

The codegen path is the fast/ergonomic default, but it doesn't fit when the
schema is only available at runtime: multi-tenant SaaS where each tenant
uploads their own schema, admin tools that pretty-print arbitrary messages,
GraphQL-fragment-shaped requests where the field set varies per call.

`capnwasm/dynamic` takes a schema descriptor as plain data and reads
messages without a build step. The descriptor is the same `_FIELDS` shape
codegen emits — a build pipeline that strips a generated reader to its
`_FIELDS` can feed it straight to `openDynamic`.

```js
const User = defineSchema({
  name:   { kind: "text",   slot: 0 },
  age:    { kind: "uint32", offset: 0 },
  active: { kind: "bool",   bitOffset: 32 },
});
const reader = openDynamic(cpp, User, bytes);
reader.pick(["name", "age"]);   // batched, one wasm call
reader.fields.name;             // Proxy-style single-field access
```

Three access modes: `pick(names)` for batched reads (one wasm boundary
crossing regardless of field count), `get(name)` for single fields, and a
Proxy on `.fields` for ergonomic dot access. Supported kinds: text, data,
uint8/16/32, int8/16/32, int64, uint64, float32/64, bool. Lists and nested
structs aren't in this pass; codegen still wins for those.

Tests round-trip the conformance schema's Primitives type against the
codegen reader and assert identical output. 109 tests pass total (101
previous + 8 dynamic).

Cold start: 11 ms → 0.6 ms in Node, smaller wasm everywhere

The dominant cost was the JS loader, not the wasm. Profiling showed
WebAssembly.compile + instantiate of the 109 KB slim wasm takes only ~0.5 ms
in Node. CapnCpp.load was sitting at ~11 ms because `instanceof Response`
triggered Node's lazy undici init. Switched to duck-typing — never reach
for `Response`/`Request`/`URL` unless the source is clearly fetchable.

Plus three smaller pieces:

- Drop `--export-dynamic` from the wasm link, keeping only the explicit
  `--export=cpp_*` list. Removes 13 unused C++ ABI symbols from the export
  table.
- Override KJ_REQUIRE / KJ_ASSERT macros to elide the stringified condition
  and message text. File/line info is preserved (one filename per .c++ TU,
  deduplicated). Saves ~7.8 KB raw / ~2 KB gz.
- Update the honest-comparison row and follow-ups doc with measured
  fresh-process numbers.

| | before | after |
|---|---|---|
| Node fresh-process load | ~11 ms | 0.6 ms |
| Slim wasm gzip | 41 KB | 39 KB |
| Full wasm gzip | 47 KB | 45 KB |

All 101 tests pass. Bench numbers unchanged on hot paths.

Add docs/notes-from-the-trenches.md — perf findings, including a
negative SIMD result

Single readable narrative pulling together the technical learnings from
this push:

  1. The "ASCII fast-path" loop was 30× slower than TextDecoder.decode
     above 4 KB. Removing it dropped 64 KB text echo from 1076 → 110 µs.
  2. 70% of CPU was in `calloc` from MallocMessageBuilder. Placement-
     new with a borrowed first segment killed it: tiny u8 17.7 → 8.5 µs.
  3. V8 hidden-class polymorphism on the question record cost ~1 µs per
     Return. makeQ() factory normalizes the shape: cap-passing flipped
     from 0.95× to 1.02× of capnweb.
  4. GC was 16% of CPU in burst workloads; q-record freelist (cap 256)
     cut it. Burst 1000: 2.76 → 2.48 µs/call.
  5. Pre-built 44-byte Finish frame template (questionId at byte 36)
     replaces a wasm boundary call + MallocMessageBuilder cycle.

Plus a SIMD experiment with a negative result, documented because that
*is* the interesting content for an internal post:

  baseline (-Oz, no SIMD):           41.0 KB gz, 8.96 µs tiny u8
  -Oz + simd128:                     41.2 KB gz, 8.85 µs (within noise)
  -O3 + simd128 + relaxed-simd:      49.7 KB gz, 8.25 µs  (+22% bundle
                                     for sub-µs perf gain that
                                     disappears behind any network)

Why SIMD doesn't help: everything that *would* benefit (memcpy,
TextDecoder, TextEncoder) is already SIMD-accelerated by V8 internals.
The remaining hot work is sequential pointer-following and integer
loads — no parallel arithmetic for SIMD to chew on. Reverted; the
negative result anchors the "I tried this and here's why" story.

README: add burst throughput row — 3.2x faster than capnweb at 1000-call batches

Pool question records to cut GC pressure on burst workloads

CPU profile of 1000-call bursts showed GC at 16% of total time —
the highest single bucket. Each call's q-record was a fresh
allocation that lived from #questions.set to #questions.delete
(typically <1 ms) and then became garbage. At thousands of calls per
second that's enough churn to keep young-gen GC busy.

Add a freelist (capped at 256 entries) and reuse q-records when a
question completes. The fields are reset to null so old references
don't leak.

Pattern: object pooling. Same shape as Linux's slab allocator,
Netty's ByteBufPool, sync.Pool in Go. Trade a tiny bit of complexity
for steady-state allocation rate.

Bench: burst 1000 u8 echo per-call latency 2.76 → 2.48 µs (11%
faster). Sequential workloads see a smaller win because they don't
allocate fast enough to trigger GC.

Normalize question-record shape so V8 sees one hidden class

The bootstrap and call paths were creating q-records with different
field sets:
  bootstrap: { deferred, kind, bootstrapCap }
  call:      { deferred, kind } or { deferred, kind, resultsReader, extract }

V8's hidden-class transitions made #handleReturn's q.extract / q.kind
access sites polymorphic, dropping them out of the inline-cache fast
path. makeQ() constructs every q with the same {deferred, kind,
bootstrapCap, resultsReader, extract} shape — fields default to
undefined when not used, but the hidden class is identical.

Pattern: V8 / SpiderMonkey / TurboFan all reward monomorphic call
sites. Same shape as JIT polymorphic inline caches in HotSpot or
LuaJIT — uniformity at object creation pays off at every read.

Bench: cap-passing flipped from 0.95x to 1.02x of capnweb (now beats
it). Other workloads moved a few percent too — within noise individually
but consistent across runs.

Build Finish frames in JS from a 44-byte template instead of via wasm

Finish messages are fixed-shape: every Finish for any question is the
same 44 bytes except for the questionId at byte 36 (LE u32). Patch a
JS-side Uint8Array template instead of crossing the wasm boundary,
placement-new'ing a MallocMessageBuilder, building the rpc.capnp Finish
struct, and finalizing.

Pattern: same shape as TCP using a fixed ACK template, or DNS replies
where most of the response is canned. Skip the wasm round-trip when
you already know the bytes.

Saves a wasm crossing (~6 ns) plus the wasm-side build work
(~300 ns) on every successful RPC reply, where Finish is sent right
after the Return. Noisy in bench numbers but the work is genuinely gone.

FrameReader.next: read length prefix inline + skip empty-rest subarray

next() was allocating a DataView and a "rest" Uint8Array on every
incoming frame even when the buffer ended cleanly. CPU profile showed
FrameReader.next at 8% in tight RPC loops.

Two small wins:
  - Read u32 LE via shift/or instead of DataView.getUint32 (no
    DataView allocation)
  - When the buffer ends right after the payload (the common case for
    in-process and well-formed framed transports), skip the rest
    subarray and just clear the chunk list

Bench delta is small/noisy on already-fast tiny calls but the work
is genuinely gone.
Update RPC perf table — we're now consistently faster than capnweb across the size range

Arena reuse: placement-new MessageBuilder into static storage with a
pre-allocated first segment. Eliminates calloc/free per RPC frame.

CPU profile showed 70% of wasm time in func[21] — calloc, called by
MallocMessageBuilder's segment allocator on every Bootstrap/Call/Return/
Finish/Release. Each tiny RPC was malloc + zero-fill + free of a fresh
segment.

Cross-domain pattern: object pooling. Linux slab allocator, Netty's
ByteBufPool, Node's Buffer pool — same shape. Reuse the buffer instead
of bouncing through the allocator.

MallocMessageBuilder has a constructor that takes a borrowed first
segment via kj::ArrayPtr<word>. The destructor zeroes a borrowed
segment but doesn't free it, so we can:

  - Stash a static word[8192] (any_builder) and word[512] (rpc_builder)
  - Placement-new the builder into static char[sizeof(...)] storage
  - On reset: in-place destructor (zeroes the segment) + placement-new

For payloads larger than the pre-allocated segment, MallocMessageBuilder
falls back to malloc'd overflow segments — correctness preserved.

Also lazy pipelineCap (RpcCap allocation deferred until caller reaches
for .cap). Not the bulk of the win but cleaner.

RPC bench (vs capnweb in-process):

  workload              before      after      vs capnweb
  tiny u8 echo          17.7 µs →  8.5 µs   ↑ 1.75x faster
  small 16B text        15.9 µs →  6.9 µs   ↑ 1.17x faster
  medium 256B text      14.3 µs →  5.5 µs   ↑ 1.44x faster
  large 4KB text        21.6 µs → 16.8 µs   ↑ 1.54x faster
  xl 64KB text         110.0 µs → 96.3 µs   ↑ 3.79x faster

We're now consistently faster than capnweb across the size range,
including tiny calls where the wasm boundary cost was supposed to
make us structurally slower.

Always use TextDecoder for text reads — premature ASCII fast-path was a pessimization

The codegen and LazyReader both had a hand-rolled "ASCII fast path"
loop intended to skip TextDecoder setup overhead for short strings:

  let s = "";
  for (let i = 0; i < bytes.length; i++) s += String.fromCharCode(bytes[i]);

Measured against a shared TextDecoder.decode:

  size       loop          TextDecoder    ratio
  4 B        23 ns         46 ns          loop wins
  16 B       98 ns         50 ns          tied
  256 B      968 ns        65 ns          15x slower
  4 KB       13 µs         413 ns         30x slower
  64 KB      305 µs        4.1 µs         75x slower

Crossover is ~12 bytes. For everything above that — i.e. the case
that matters in real workloads — TextDecoder wins by orders of
magnitude. Drop the loop, always go through the shared decoder.

RPC bench delta on text round-trips (vs capnweb):

  256 B    18.8 µs → 14.3 µs    (was 0.40x, now 0.54x of capnweb)
  4 KB     71.8 µs → 21.6 µs    (was 0.36x, now 1.44x — beats capnweb)
  64 KB    1076 µs → 110 µs     (was 0.37x, now 3.46x — 10x faster)

The cross-domain pattern: premature optimization without measurement.
The loop was meant to dodge TextDecoder's per-call cost, but
TextDecoder.decode is internal C++ that crushes any JS loop above a
handful of bytes. Same shape as people manually unrolling loops the
compiler vectorizes better.

Drop /slim entrypoint — browser entrypoint already uses the slim wasm

capnwasm/slim was the inlined production-only single-file bundle
(~60 KB gz) — the same wasm as capnwasm/browser but base64-encoded
into the JS. Now that capnwasm/browser exists and ships the same
slim wasm as a separate fetched asset (~44 KB gz, 30% smaller, with
streaming compile), /slim sits in an awkward middle ground:

  - Browser users want /browser (smaller + streaming compile)
  - Node users want the default capnwasm (single file, all features)
  - /slim only wins for "inlined and small but can't ship a separate
    .wasm asset" — a niche that doesn't justify a third bundle

Removed the export, the build step that emits dist/inlined.slim.mjs,
and the --slim flag in build_inlined.mjs. capnp.slim.wasm is still
produced (it's what /browser fetches).

Cache wasm exports + Uint8Array view in generated Builder/Reader

Every per-field setter/getter was doing two property-chain reads
(this._cpp._exports.cpp_*) plus a fresh Uint8Array allocation
(this._cpp._u8 returns new Uint8Array(buffer) each time). With many
fields per struct that adds up — a hot loop that builds 17 fields
allocates 17+ throwaway typed-arrays per iteration.

Cache cpp._exports as this._exp and (for the Builder) the Uint8Array
view as this._u8 in the constructor. Primitive setters then store
straight through this._u8[off] with no per-call allocation; getters
read this._exp.cpp_*() with one less property-chain hop.

Subtlety: text/data setters and toBytes() can grow wasm memory
(MallocMessageBuilder allocates new segments), which detaches any
typed-array view captured beforehand. Those paths fetch a fresh
view via this._cpp._u8 and then refresh this._u8 so subsequent
primitive setters see a live buffer. Reader text/data getters follow
the same fetch-fresh-on-grow rule but skip caching since they're
the only readers that touch the view.

All 81 tests still green. Bench numbers don't move much in the RPC
hot path (wasm work dominates), but a 256-field primitive write loop
(BigUser) shows a measurable drop in GC pressure.

Drop redundant defensive copy in createMemoryTransportPair

makeEnd().send was doing bytes.slice() before queueing delivery, but
RpcSession#sendFromOut already produces JS-owned bytes by slicing out
of wasm memory. The transport-layer copy was just memcpy'ing those
bytes again — pure overhead, especially at 64 KB+ payload sizes.

Bench delta on xl 64KB text echo: ~40 µs faster per round-trip.

Pack 3 Return-side accessor calls into one boundary call

cpp_rpc_get_return_summary writes answerId + retKind + capCount packed
into cpp_out so JS reads them with a single DataView. Mirrors the
existing cpp_rpc_get_call_summary pattern. Saves 2 wasm-boundary
crossings on every Return — small (~100 ns/call) but it's free, the
code is cleaner, and consistent with the Call path.

Split runtime wasi shim out of the codegen one

The capnp runtime wasm only declares 5 wasi imports. The full shim
covered ~25 (filesystem, environ, clock, random) for the schema
compiler's libc init paths. Inlining the full shim into every runtime
bundle was wasted bytes.

Pull the runtime imports into js/cpp_wasi_runtime.mjs and have
cpp_loader/build_inlined point at it. The codegen path keeps using
js/cpp_wasi_shim.mjs which now imports the runtime base + adds the
compiler-only stubs on top.

Bundle deltas:
  inlined.slim.mjs  61 KB → 60 KB gzip
  inlined.mjs       69 KB → 68 KB gzip
  capnwasm/browser  45 KB → 44 KB gzip

Drop unused cpp/schema.capnp.c++ from runtime source list

wrapper.cpp doesn't reference any capnp::schema:: symbols, and rpc.capnp
doesn't import schema.capnp at the source level — LTO + --gc-sections
were already DCE'ing the file, so the wasm output is byte-identical.
This is a build-speed cleanup: ~780 lines of generated accessors no
longer have to round-trip through compile + LTO before being thrown
away.
Update README single-call latency: 17µs → 15µs after caching wins

Cache wasm scratch-buffer offsets + memory view in RpcSession

cpp_in_ptr / cpp_out_ptr / cpp_in_capacity are getters for fixed C++
globals — they return the same value on every call. The session was
re-querying them per-send (and allocating a fresh Uint8Array view on
every #stageIn / #sendFromOut / #snapshotOut). Cache them once at
construction; re-fetch the Uint8Array only if memory.buffer identity
changes (memory.grow, which our wasm doesn't trigger in practice).

Saves ~2-3μs per round-trip. Single-call u8 echo measured before:
17μs, after: 14.6μs.

Add capnwasm/browser entrypoint: separate-wasm + streaming compile

For browser users, capnwasm/browser ships the wasm as a fetched asset
(dist/capnp.slim.wasm) instead of base64-inlining it into a JS string.
Two wins:

- Smaller. Total transfer drops from 64 KB gz / 61 KB br
  (capnwasm/slim, inlined) to 48 KB gz / 44 KB br
  (capnwasm/browser + separate .wasm) — base64 was inflating the
  inlined form by ~33% before gzip recovered most.

- Faster cold start. CapnCpp.load() now uses
  WebAssembly.instantiateStreaming when given a URL/Request/Response,
  so the wasm starts compiling while bytes are still arriving. The
  Uint8Array path is unchanged — existing inlined.mjs/inlined.slim.mjs
  callers see no behavior change.

Test coverage: 4 new tests in test/browser_loader.test.mjs spin up an
HTTP server and verify the streaming path actually fires (file:// +
Node's fetch don't compose).

Always microtask-batch sends; cache wasm exports on session

Previously had three batching configurations (off / microtask / time-window)
exposed via { batching, batchIntervalMs } options + a newBatchedRpcSession
factory + a (briefly) batchingTransport wrapper. This created multiple
code paths that the user had to choose between, and even when they chose
'off' the batching infrastructure was still sitting in the bundle as
dead weight.

Simplified to one path: RpcSession always microtask-batches. The cost is
≤ one microtask of first-byte latency (~1µs, invisible behind any
network); the win is N→1 transport.send calls when the user makes
multiple calls in the same tick. session.flush() exists for the rare
case you want to force a send before the boundary.

Removed: newBatchedRpcSession, options.batching, options.batchIntervalMs,
setBatching(). Updated test + bench to drop the batched/non-batched
columns; one workload, one number, no config to second-guess.

Also cached cpp._exports as RpcSession.#exp to shorten hot-path property
chains. No measurable perf delta in the bench (V8 was already inlining
the access) but the code reads cleaner.

77/77 tests still green.

RPC + REST + OpenAPI codegen + browser-runnable schema compiler

Capability surface added on top of the runtime:

- RPC layer (js/rpc.mjs): full Cap'n Proto RPC with promise pipelining,
  capability passing with FinalizationRegistry-driven Release, server-side
  refcount tracking, opt-in batching (newBatchedRpcSession), streaming via
  AsyncIterable over a custom STREAM_CHUNK/STREAM_END frame extension,
  WebSocket transport adapter, and an in-memory transport pair for tests.

- Zero-copy data path: cap.callBuilder + ctx.beginResults write/read
  directly into rpc_builder/rpc_reader arenas; cpp_rpc_finalize emits
  segment table + segments straight to cpp_out (no messageToFlatArray
  allocation). encodeInto for text setters writes UTF-8 into wasm memory
  with no JS allocation. Single-digit B/call JS heap regardless of
  payload size.

- Wasm-built capnp schema compiler (cpp/capnpc_wrapper.cpp +
  cpp/build_capnpc.sh): the upstream compiler statically linked into
  dist/capnpc.wasm with standard schemas (c++.capnp, schema.capnp, rpc.capnp,
  etc.) baked in via cpp/embed_standard_schemas.sh. Replaces the unsafe
  CAPNP_BIN env-var fallback. dist/codegen.mjs is a single-file inlined
  bundle that runs in any browser with zero host I/O.

- Codegen completeness: List<X> getters (typed list view with length / at(i) /
  iterator) for primitives, Text, Data, and struct elements; nested
  group/union with which() / isFoo() / discriminant-gated property
  accessors and Builder counterparts (auto-set discriminant, setWhich(),
  Which.* constants); group fields surface as r.parent.child via
  synthesized nested Reader/Builder classes that share the parent's
  storage.

- REST codegen (capnwasm gen on .ts files with @rest interfaces):
  parses @get/@post/@put/@patch/@delete + @query/@header/@body/@paginated
  directives, emits a typed fetch-based client. Runtime in
  js/rest_runtime.mjs: bearer/apiKey/basic/custom auth, retries with
  exponential/linear backoff + Retry-After honoring, AbortSignal
  cancellation, JSON/multipart/form/raw body encodings, AsyncIterable
  cursor + page pagination, RestError with parsed body, request/response
  /error interceptors.

- OpenAPI 3.x codegen (capnwasm openapi <spec>): walks paths/operations
  + components.schemas + securitySchemes, $ref resolution including
  allOf/oneOf/anyOf, enum/nullable/array, security inference, tag-based
  grouping. Tested against a real-world Petstore spec.

- Bundle structure: dist/inlined.mjs (full, default), dist/inlined.slim.mjs
  (production-only opt-in via "capnwasm/slim"), dist/codegen.mjs
  (compiler bundle), dist/capnpc.wasm (linkable separately).
  Test-only schemas (typed/big/conformance) gated behind CW_BENCH=1.

- 77 tests across runtime, RPC, REST, OpenAPI, browser, streaming, lists,
  groups, unions, auto-release, capabilities, pipelining. All green.

- Bench infra: bench/scale-runner.mjs (seconds-scale workloads),
  bench/rpc-runner.mjs (browser RPC), bench/realistic.mjs (Node
  burst/wire-bytes), bench/rpc_bench.mjs (Node RPC vs capnweb).

Upgrade WASM build to Zig 0.16

Two changes needed to get gitmode-core.wasm building cleanly with Zig 0.16:

1. build.zig API: Compile.addObjectFile / addIncludePath were removed in
   0.16 — they're now methods on Build.Module. Redirect through
   `wasm.root_module.addObjectFile(...)` to match how addLibdeflate
   already attaches sources via the module rather than the Compile step.

2. main_core.zig was missing heapSave/heapRestore exports — only main.zig
   (the full module) defined them. The pre-allocated scratch ABI in
   wasm-engine-core.ts requires heapSave at startup; the latent gap had
   been silently carried forward through previous rebuilds because no
   test exercised WasmEngineCore.create() until this session. Adding
   matching exports unblocks the lightweight engine.

Rebuilt both binaries with `mise exec zig@0.16.0 -- pnpm build:wasm-all`:

Git protocol v2 support: ls-refs and fetch commands

Adds v2 to smart-http.ts alongside the existing v1 implementation.
Modern Git CLIs default to v2; this rounds out the protocol coverage.

V2 negotiation:
- discoverRefs sends `Git-Protocol: version=2` header by default
- If the server response begins with `version 2\n`, parse capabilities
  and follow up with a v2 ls-refs POST
- Otherwise fall back to v1 ref-advertisement parsing
- protocolVersion: "v1" forces the old path (skips the v2 header entirely)
- protocolVersion: "v2" forces v2 (no fallback)

V2 ls-refs:
  POST /git-upload-pack with body:
    command=ls-refs\n agent=…\n object-format=sha1\n
    <delim> peel\n symrefs\n ref-prefix HEAD\n ref-prefix refs/heads/\n …
    <flush>
  Response: <sha> <ref> [symref-target:<target>] [peeled:<sha>]\n …

V2 fetch:
  POST /git-upload-pack with body:
    command=fetch\n agent=…\n object-format=sha1\n
    <delim> ofs-delta\n want <sha>\n … done\n
    <flush>
  Response: packfile\n + sideband-multiplexed pack data + flush
  (sideband-all is implicit in v2; no need to opt in via capability)

V2 demux differs from v1: v1 has a NAK/ACK preamble before pack data,
v2 has explicit section headers ("packfile\n"). demuxSidebandV2 handles
this and skips other section headers (acknowledgments, shallow-info)
that aren't relevant for a basic fetch.

- falls back to v1 when server doesn't advertise v2
- protocolVersion: "v1" skips v2 negotiation

- v2 sideband channel separation (progress / errors)

prompt-blame: add /prompt-text endpoints + CLI commands

Closes a documented gap. The schema reserved a prompt_text table since
the initial migration but no endpoint exposed it; the README claimed

Privacy-aware design (matches what the README always promised):
- Default behavior is unchanged: commit_metadata never auto-stores prompt
  text. Callers who want it must explicitly POST to /prompt-text.
- Treats prompt_id as content-addressed (typically a hash). Mismatched
  text for an existing prompt_id is rejected rather than overwriting.
- 64KB cap keeps D1 happy; larger prompts would go to R2 (deferred).

CLI:

- prompt-blame get-prompt --prompt-id=…

blameWalk coordinator + shared pool-rpc helpers

Third advertised coordinator. Per-line attribution via first-parent
history walk: resolve file path through nested trees with walk-trees,
read content with read-blobs, compare line-set with each ancestor,
push attribution back as long as the line is still present. Final
attribution = oldest commit where the line still appears.

POC quality limitations (documented in module header):
  - First-parent walk only — merges follow one branch

read-blobs pool action: pure blob content read with size cap

Seventh PackWorkerDO action. Decompresses blob objects from R2 (via
the existing readRawObject helper that uses WasmEngine zlib), strips
the `blob <size>\0` header, validates type, returns base64 content
+ size capped at maxBlobBytes (default 1MB, hard max 8MB).

Why base64 in JSON: returning binary content via fetch+JSON requires
encoding. Buffer.from(...).toString("base64") (nodejs_compat) avoids
the call-stack overflow you'd hit with String.fromCharCode spread.

Why a size cap: the action runs in a ~128MB DO; without a cap a single
oversized blob could OOM the slot. Caller can raise maxBlobBytes per
request when the blobs are known to be small.

Unblocks future coordinators that need raw blob content (blameWalk
needs to diff old vs new blob bytes, custom-grep needs the bytes
exposed rather than just match results).

logWalk coordinator: BFS with arbitrary filter predicate

Second of three advertised coordinators (mergeBase shipped previously,
blameWalk deferred). Walks history breadth-first from seed SHAs,
including a commit in results when the filter predicate returns true,
either way queueing its parents for the next level.

Common usages: filter by author email, by date range, or by message
regex (the `git log -S "TODO"` pattern). 6 integration tests cover
all reachable, empty seeds, limit cap, author filter, message regex,
and maxDepth termination.

mergeBase coordinator: BFS atop parse-commits

Demonstrates the toolkit composition pattern. mergeBase walks the
commit graph from two seed SHAs, dispatching one parse-commits RPC
per BFS level per side. Both sides walk in parallel via Promise.all,
so a single coordinator saturates two slots even at minimum dispatch
granularity.

API surface (in src/coordinators/merge-base.ts, exported from index):
  mergeBase({ shaA, shaB, repoPath, lookup, pool, maxDepth? })
    where lookup(sha) returns the R2 location (chunkKey/offset/looseKey)

Caller owns SHA-to-location resolution because the coordinator stays
storage-layout-agnostic — works against gitmode's chunk index, against
loose objects, or against any custom layout.

Tests cover six topology cases:
  - equal SHAs return self
  - ancestor relationship returns the parent
  - sibling commits return their common parent
  - deep ancestry walks several levels
  - disjoint histories return null
  - maxDepth cap returns null instead of looping

Pivot: gitmode → POC toolkit on Cloudflare Artifacts

Cloudflare shipped Artifacts (April 16, 2026) with the same architecture
this repo had — Worker + DO + Zig WASM git server. Continuing as a
parallel implementation made no sense, especially given the maintainer
works at Cloudflare. Pivoted to an OSS toolkit on top of Artifacts,
explicitly framed as a POC for learning rather than a product.

What's in:
- packages/prompt-blame   — D1 sidecar + Worker + CLI for capturing
                             prompt/session/model/agent provenance per
                             commit; composes with ../timeline.
- packages/edge-compute-pool — PackWorkerDO with 6 fan-out actions
                             (build-segment, write-worktree, diff-blobs,
                             grep-blobs, walk-trees, parse-commits).
- packages/wasm-git       — Zig+WASM engines extracted as a library.

What's out:
- The full git server (src/, app/, worker/, ssh/, scripts/, bin/, test/)
  ~13.6k lines deleted across 60 files.
- npm publish, launch blog, repo rename — all dropped per POC framing.

47 tests pass across the three packages; typecheck clean. Strategy and
phase log live in DESIGN-NOTES.md; conflict-of-interest disclosure in
DISCLAIMER.md.
